center.title: US signs landmark nuclear deal with Saudi Arabia

farLeftText: From 6h ago 19.34 EDT Hearing echoes of US military support for Shah of Iran, Democratic congressman calls for hearings on Saudi nuclear deal At House foreign affairs committee hearing on Wednesday, congressman Brad Sherman, a Los Angeles Democrat, called for an oversight hearing of the Trump administration’s nuclear cooperation agreement with Saudi Arabia, suggesting that exporting that technology could be as big a mistake as having armed the Shah of Iran in the 1970s. “There are those who think that the Saudi possession of nuclear technology is totally benign, but I’ve been involved in this for a long time, and I remember when the most powerful monarch in the Middle East was a good friend of America. And the Shah got his hands on important US military technology. Thank God, nothing nuclear,” Sherman said while questioning Mike Waltz, the US ambassador to the UN, and former national security adviser to Donald Trump. “Everything the Shah had in 1978 was in the mullahs’ hands in 1979,” Sherman said, referring to the Islamic revolution that toppled the Iranian monarch and brought the theocratic regime to power. “We have no idea who is going to be running Saudi Arabia 10 years from now.” Video of congressman Brad Sherman questioning UN ambassador Mike Waltz on Wednesday at a House foreign affairs committee hearing. Sherman went onto point out that, in 2018, he and then-senator Marco Rubio had introduced the “No Nuclear Weapons for Saudi Arabia Act” which would have required approval from Congress before the US could enter into a civilian nuclear cooperation agreement with Saudi Arabia, and a Saudi investigation into the death of journalist Jamal Khashoggi - whose murder the same year in Turkey US intelligence later determined had been approved by the Saudi crown prince, Mohammed bin Salman. Sherman argued that Congress should approve the deal only if it has what he called later “the same gold-standard safeguards as our deal with the UAE,” referring to a similar agreement with the United Arab Emirates struck in 2009, in which the UAE voluntarily committed to nonproliferation and agreed to not pursue domestic uranium enrichment and reprocessing. “Is there any reason we should be bombing Iran because they won’t agree to limit their nuclear program while telling Saudi Arabia we give them the green light and they don’t even have to agree to these additional inspections?” Sherman asked Waltz. After Waltz argued that US resolutions calling on Iran to not develop nuclear weapons provided a pretext for the current US-Israeli war on Iran, Sherman suggested there was “great hypocrisy here.” “Saudi Arabia is not agreeing to the additional inspections that could make sure that their program is not used … to develop a nuclear weapon,” the congressman said, based on press reports of the terms of the agreement that has not yet been shared with Congress. “I predict someone will introduce a resolution of disapproval and I invite members of this committee who have worked with us to prevent nuclear weapons in the Middle East, from non-democratic countries, to join in that effort,” Sherman said, in an oblique reference to the open secret that one nation in the region, Israel, does have nuclear weapons. Share Updated at 19.50 EDT 4h ago 22.00 EDT Closing summary This concludes our live coverage of the second Trump administration for the day. Here are the latest developments: The Trump administration signed a nuclear agreement with Saudi Arabia on Wednesday that could enable the kingdom to enrich its own uranium for nuclear reactors. At a House foreign affairs committee hearing, congressman Brad Sherman , a Los Angeles Democrat, called for an oversight hearing of the Trump administration’s nuclear cooperation agreement with Saudi Arabia, suggesting that exporting that technology could be as big a mistake as having armed the Shah of Iran in the 1970s. The US House of Representatives passed a $1.15tn defense policy bill in a near-party-line vote, after Democrats refused to back the measure amid the war with Iran and Donald Trump’s push to impose new restrictions on voting nationwide. During his 75-minute address to supporters in Georgia, Trump took some time out from boasting about what he calls his many accomplishments to attack Jon Ossoff , the incumbent Democratic senator he referred to, confusingly, as “Pinky Herman”. As the US strikes again targets in Iran in retaliation for that nation’s blockade of the strait of Hormuz, Iran-backed Houthi rebels in Yemen claim to have attacked two Saudi ⁠oil tankers for violating a blockade near the Bab el-Mandeb Strait, a chokepoint for oil exports transiting the Red Sea. Share Updated at 22.42 EDT 4h ago 21.54 EDT Michigan Senate candidate Abdul El-Sayed does have Black support, AOC says, after Clyburn suggests he does not Alexandria Ocasio-Cortez, the progressive New York congresswoman, denied on Wednesday that the Michigan Senate candidate she supports, Abdul El-Sayed, lacks support from Black voters. The congresswoman was asked to respond to a comment her Democratic colleague James Clyburn made to the outlet Punchbowl about images of an El-Sayed rally in Detroit on Saturday that she and senator Bernie Sanders spoke at. Local news photographs, social-media images and video of the rally at the Detroit Opera House shared online showed that the crowd, in a city that is 75% Black, was largely white. “I saw a picture that concerned me coming out of Michigan. I saw the rally,” Clyburn told Punchbowl. “I didn’t see a single person that looks like me. That tells me all I need to know.” Some of the Black voters who did attend the rally told Sam Robinson, a Black independent journalist, that they were not troubled by online criticism of a majority white crowd in a majority Black city supporting El-Sayed. Denzel McCampbell, a Black member of Detroit’s city council who is campaigning for El-Sayed, dismissed what he called “a narrative about this campaign, about Abdul” created by “folks on Twitter”, and insisted that he has seen support for the candidate while knocking on doors in the city. “His message is something that resonates with folks across a broad spectrum,” McCampbell said. At least one recent poll suggests that El-Sayed’s rival in the Democratic primary for Senate, congresswoman Haley Stevens, leads with Black voters. She is also supported by at least a dozen members of the Congressional Black Caucus, including Clyburn. “I think when you look on the ground, Abdul El-Sayed enjoys the support locally of many Black elected leaders and Black community leaders throughout the state of Michigan,” Ocasio-Cortez told reporters on Wednesday. “I also think the community is nuanced. I don’t believe that it’s a monolith. I think, just like many other communities, there are generational dynamics that are to be considered,” she added. “So we’ll see as the race plays out.” One of the rally speakers before El-Sayed was a Black elected official: Donavan McKinney, a Democratic Socialist state lawmaker who is running for Congress. View image in fullscreen Donavan McKinney, Alexandria Ocasio-Cortez, Abdul El-Sayed, Bernie Sanders, Shawn Fain and Rashida Tlaib on stage at a campaign rally for El-Sayed in the Detroit Opera House on 18 July. Photograph: Jose Juarez/AP Clyburn’s comments come amid a struggle inside the Democratic party to decide which states should go first in the 2028 presidential primary campaign, with his home state of South Carolina vying with states including New Hampshire, Nevada and Michigan to hold the first primary. Share Updated at 22.51 EDT 5h ago 20.41 EDT UAE agreed not to enrich uranium in nuclear deal with Obama; will Trump's deal with Saudis be different? On its face, the nuclear cooperation agreement, commonly known as a 123 agreement (after the relevant section of the US Atomic Energy Act) signed on Wednesday by the Trump administration and Saudi Arabia looks similar to an agreement signed in 2009 by the Obama administration and the United Arab Emirates. However, until we see the text, it is not clear if the deals are alike in one key way: whether or not the Saudis have agreed, as the UAE did, to safeguards that would block the kingdom from developing nuclear weapons. In the UAE deal, first negotiated during the Bush administration, and then reworked and signed by the Obama administration on 21 May, the Emirates agreed to accept the International Atomic Energy Agency’s so-called “additional protocol”, which allows the agency to verify the peaceful use of all nuclear material, and affirmed its commitment “to pursue a renunciation of the development of domestic enrichment and reprocessing capabilities in favor of long-term commitments for the secure external supply of nuclear fuel”. “In signing this Agreement, the UAE has demonstrated its commitment to develop civil nuclear energy responsibly, in full conformity with its nonproliferation commitments and obligations,” the Obama state department wrote in a 2009 fact sheet. “Among those is the UAE’s commitment not to pursue enrichment and reprocessing (ENR) within its territory, representing a marked contrast to Iran which has failed to comply with its international obligations.” Initial reports on the terms of the Trump administration’s deal with Saudi Arabia suggest that it could allow for uranium enrichment in Saudi Arabia, opening a potential pathway to the production of nuclear weapons in the kingdom. “The deal breaks from the ‘gold standard’ of nonproliferation, which would prohibit domestic uranium enrichment and plutonium reprocessing,” the Wall Street Journal editorial board argued on Wednesday, under the headline: “Saudi Nuclear Enrichment Is a Mistake”. The Journal’s editors went on to suggest that Trump’s Saudi nuclear deal will have weaker safeguards than the Bush-Obama UAE nuclear deal: double quotation mark The next best way to prevent nuclear proliferation is with inspections and monitoring, but the Trump deal weakens those, too. The tried-and-true method is the International Atomic Energy Agency’s Additional Protocol. The United Arab Emirates signed up for it, along with the gold standard of no domestic enrichment, as a condition of its 2009 nuclear deal negotiated largely with George W. Bush. But now the Saudis have been allowed to reject the Additional Protocol and receive U.S. support all the same. The Trump Administration says it has devised a bilateral safeguards agreement of its own. But we doubt the Saudis rejected the standard protocol in favor of even stricter inspections. They are bound to be weaker, establishing the precedent that each U.S. ally can push for its own preferential monitoring. Share Updated at 20.46 EDT 6h ago 19.49 EDT George Chidi Donald Trump scoffed at the word “affordability” as a political message, describing it at a campaign rally on Wednesday as a term concocted by Democratic political consultants to criticize his presidency. Yet even as the president dismissed the notion of affordability, he devoted much of his speech at Wheeler high school to touting the bread-and-butter benefits of his economic policies. “So I just want you to remember that if you’re paying a little bit more – a lot of it’s come down now, we got it coming down very rapidly actually,” Trump said in Marietta, an Atlanta suburb, casting higher prices today as a lingering effect of the Biden administration. Americans are broadly disappointed with Trump’s handling of the economy, with just 33% of voters approving in a recent Washington Post-Ipsos poll, an assessment that could hurt his party’s hopes of bucking historical precedent and retaining control of Congress in November’s midterm elections. Trump scoffs at ‘affordability’ as he touts economic policies at Georgia rally Read more Share 6h ago 19.39 EDT Bernie Sanders calls deal to provide nuclear technology to Saudi Arabia 'absurd' Senator Bernie Sanders, a Vermont independent, expressed strong opposition to the Trump administration’s deal to help Saudi Arabia enrich uranium for nuclear energy, a capability that could open the door to the repressive kingdom developing nuclear weapons. “Trump claims his war on Iran is about stopping an authoritarian country from enriching nuclear fuel,” Sanders wrote on social media. “Now he’s letting his best friends in Saudi Arabia — one of the most brutal dictatorships in the world — do exactly that. This is absurd. No nuclear for the Saudi dictatorship.” Share 6h ago 19.34 EDT Hearing echoes of US military support for Shah of Iran, Democratic congressman calls for hearings on Saudi nuclear deal At House foreign affairs committee hearing on Wednesday, congressman Brad Sherman, a Los Angeles Democrat, called for an oversight hearing of the Trump administration’s nuclear cooperation agreement with Saudi Arabia, suggesting that exporting that technology could be as big a mistake as having armed the Shah of Iran in the 1970s. “There are those who think that the Saudi possession of nuclear technology is totally benign, but I’ve been involved in this for a long time, and I remember when the most powerful monarch in the Middle East was a good friend of America. Share Updated at 19.50 EDT 7h ago 18.41 EDT George Chidi At a Donald Trump event in Marietta, Georgia, the state’s Republican candidate for governor Rick Jackson pledged to ask the Georgia legislature to match the $1,000 federal contribution to childhood Trump investment accounts, and if they failed to do so, would match the figure himself. “Here in Georgia, we should not stope with $1000,” Jackson said. “I believe our legislature will approve that. If they don’t I will personally donate it myself.” Jackson is the founder of Jackson Healthcare, a medical staffing company. His personal net worth has been estimated to be more than $3 billion. In 2024, the state of Georgia recorded a bit more than 126,000 live births, which equates to $126 million in annual contributions for Trump accounts. Georgia began the 2026 fiscal year with an unprecedented budget cushion of about $15 billion in the bank, with $9 billion in unrestricted funds. This surplus resulted from conservative budgeting that began in 2021 as a reaction to pandemic pressures. That money has become an intense political question, with Republicans looking for ways to provide property tax relief while Democrats arguing for a broader investment in health care and child care support. Share 7h ago 18.31 EDT As the US strikes again targets in Iran in retaliation for that nation’s blockade of the strait of Hormuz, Iran-backed Houthi rebels in Yemen claim to have attacked two Saudi ⁠oil tankers for violating a blockade near the Bab el-Mandeb Strait, a chokepoint for oil exports transiting the Red Sea. Saudi Arabia, which borders Yemen, led a military intervention in 2015, triggering a year-long civil war to prop up Yemen’s Saudi-backed government. The United Kingdom Maritime Trade Operations Centre said in an alert issued on Wednesday night it “has received a report of an incident 70NM southwest of AL SHUQAIQ, Saudi Arabia. The Master of a tanker reports being struck by an unknown projectile which has caused a fire onboard that the crew are currently fighting.” Senator Chris Murphy, a Connecticut Democrat, responded to news of the Houthi attacks by writing on social media: “Everyone saw all of this coming. This chaos was totally foreseeable and Trump chose to start this war anyway.” Share Updated at 20.54 EDT 8h ago 18.16 EDT Trump boasts about helping defeat Jon Ossoff in 2017 special election, fails to mention his role in Ossoff's Senate win in 2021 During his 75-minute address to supporters in Georgia on Wednesday, Donald Trump took some time out from boasting about what he calls his many accomplishments to attack Jon Ossoff, the incumbent Democratic senator whose withering criticism of the president has thrilled Democrats and led to calls for Ossoff to mount a 2028 presidential campaign, should he first win his reelection bid in November. “I fought Ossoff before, as you know, and we won. We had a great success against this guy,” Trump said, fondly recalling his role in Ossoff’s narrow loss in a 2017 special election in Georgia’s sixth congressional district, in what was then a Republican-dominated Atlanta suburb. “He reminds me of Pinky Herman,” the president said. “Do you ever see Pinky Herman?” Trump asked, a comment that prompted some laughter from his supporters in the hall, and a scramble, among journalists, to figure out what, exactly, he was talking about. It was not until fully thirteen minutes later, when Trump predicted that Ossoff’s Republican challenger, Mike Collins, is “going to beat Pee-wee Herman”, that the 80-year-old president’s reference to the character played by the late comic actor Paul Reubens four decades ago became clear. For a president who rose to power largely through his insult-comic hits on his political opponents, it was a peculiarly low-energy jibe, given that Ossoff, who was once as skinny as the Reubens character, has now bulked up, and speaks about the president in a tone of righteous indignation that bears no resemblance to Pee-wee’s squeaky voice. Trump made no mention at all of the prominent role he played in trying, and failing, to stop Ossoff from winning his Senate seat in a run-off election on 5 January, 2021. The night before that election, in which Democratic senator Raphael Warnock also won his seat, Trump had traveled to Georgia to speak at a rally for the two Republican candidates, senators Kelly Loeffler and David Perdue. Rather than urging voters to support the Republican candidates, Trump instead undermined confidence in the electoral system in remarks focused mainly on his claim that the presidential election just two months earlier had been stolen from him. “Hello, Georgia! By the way, there is no way we lost Georgia. There’s no way, … That was a rigged election” Trump began his speech that night. “When you win in a landslide, and they steal it, and it’s rigged, it’s not acceptable,” he told the crowd, hours before Republican turnout slumped and Ossoff and Warnock were elected, swinging control of the Senate to the Democrats. View image in fullscreen Donald Trump with then-senator Kelly Loeffler at a rally in Dalton, Georgia on 4 January 2021, the night before Republicans lost a pair of Senate runoffs as Trump claimed that elections in the state were “rigged”, despite being administered by Republican officials. Photograph: Sandy Huffaker/AFP/Getty Images Share 9h ago 17.21 EDT US announces nuclear deal with Saudi Arabia that could enable kingdom to enrich uranium The Trump administration signed a nuclear agreement with Saudi Arabia on Wednesday that could enable the kingdom to enrich its own uranium for nuclear reactors, US Energy secretary Chris Wright said in a press release. According to a statement from the Department of Energy, Wright and his Saudi counterpart, Prince Abdulaziz bin Salman, signed “a peaceful nuclear cooperation agreement” and “an accompanying bilateral safeguards agreement.” The question of what those safeguards look like, given the potential for enriched uranium to be used to make nuclear weapons, will be closely scrutinized when the agreement is sent to Congress for review. The two agreements, the press release said, “lay the legal foundation for a decades-long, multi-billion-dollar partnership that advances several priority economic and strategic objectives, including nuclear nonproliferation.” “These agreements reflect our two nations’ shared commitment to strengthening U.S.-Saudi commercial relations, delivering prosperity at home and security to our allies abroad,” Wright said in a statement. “Rest assured, these agreements uphold the highest standards of nuclear safety and nonproliferation, while relying on the world’s best nuclear technology and scientists, designed right here in the United States.” As our colleague Patrick Wintour reported earlier: double quotation mark The timing of the announcement is controversial as the US’s war with Iran is being fought in part to prevent Tehran enriching uranium domestically and make it near-impossible for Iran to build a nuclear weapon. The US-Saudi deal is expected to last 30 years and involve US firms such as Westinghouse in developing the programme. It could allow for the building of a uranium enrichment facility in Saudi Arabia after a joint US-Saudi study. The proposal has been in discussion for years. At one point the Biden administration wanted to condition Saudi Arabia’s access to the technology on normalising relations with Israel, something Saudi Arabia is still refusing to do. The plan is expected to be submitted to Congress for review, where it could face opposition among lawmakers who fear a lack of monitoring safeguards to restrict Saudi Arabia’s uranium enrichment. Share Updated at 17.28 EDT 9h ago 16.46 EDT Chris Stein The US House of Representatives passed a $1.15tn defense policy bill in a near-party-line vote on Wednesday, after Democrats refused to back the measure amid the war with Iran and Donald Trump’s push to impose new restrictions on voting nationwide. A key plank of the president’s plan to spend $1.5tn on the military in the 2027 fiscal year, the National Defense Authorization Act (NDAA), passed the Republican-controlled chamber by a 216-212 vote, with nearly all Democrats opposing its passage. The measure now goes to the Senate. Congress passes the NDAA each year with bipartisan margins, with the measure’s high cost seen as necessary to make generational investments in the military. “For the first time in 40 years, we’ve been presented a budget that accounts for the true cost of American deterrence. This topline begins to reverse the damage caused by decades of underinvestment in the US military,” Mike Rogers, the House armed services committee chair, said before the vote. But the path to enactment for this year’s NDAA has been complicated by the president’s decision to join Israel in attacking Iran without first seeking authorization from Congress. Last week, Senate Democrats blocked the advancement of that chamber’s version of the NDAA in protest of the war, which has flared anew after a memorandum of understanding meant to spur peace talks collapsed. US House passes $1.15tn defense policy bill in near party-line vote Read more Share 9h ago 16.36 EDT Trump rails against specter of 'communism' to supporters in Georgia Donald Trump is currently delivering what is supposedly a presidential address to promote a new domestic policy, namely the investment accounts for children he calls Trump accounts, but, surrounded by supporters in Marietta, Georgia, his remarks quickly became deeply partisan, repeatedly attacking Democrats and falsely claiming the party is now filled with “communists”. Trump, whose political mentor Roy Cohn led senator Joseph McCarthy’s 1950s witch hunts for imaginary communist infiltrators in the US government, made the wild claim that Democrats he called “communists by another name” want to blow up Mount Rushmore. He then proclaimed: “we declare again today that the United States of America will never be a communist country!” The crowd, led by a group of die-hard Trump supporters who call themselves Front Row Joes, for their repeated presence at his rallies, broke into a chant of “USA! USA!” View image in fullscreen Donald Trump dances after speaking at Wheeler High School in Marietta, Georgia, on 22 July. Photograph: Saul Loeb/AFP/Getty Images Share Updated at 17.05 EDT

centerText: US Secretary of State Marco Rubio pushed back against critics who may be concerned about Saudi Arabia's ability to develop a nuclear weapon in the future. "The US is not going to reach any agreement with any country in the world that leads to the risk of proliferation," Rubio told reporters while travelling in the Philippines. Several Democrats who voiced opposition to the deal noted that during his time as a senator, Rubio had endorsed a law - the "No Nuclear Weapons for Saudi Arabia Act" - which required Congress to sign off on a nuclear deal with the Saudis. Massachusetts Democratic Senator Edward Markey issued a statement condemning Rubio and Trump, and saying that their "hypocrisy is matched only by their recklessness". "They are allowing Saudi Arabia, a belligerent and authoritarian nation, to develop nuclear weapons technologies while starting a war with Iran under the guise of preventing an Iranian nuclear bomb," said Markey. If an enrichment provision is included, it would distinguish this agreement from another one that was struck by the US in 2009 with Saudi Arabia's neighbour, the United Arab Emirates, under which the UAE does not produce its own fuel and accepts a host of other restrictions. For that reason, the agreement is also expected to attract the concern of nuclear non-proliferation campaigners, whose ultimate aim is to prevent an increase in the number of nuclear weapons worldwide. Saudi Arabia has been lobbying for such a deal for years under multiple presidents, but Washington has held out with a key request that the country recognise Israel, a key US ally, and normalise diplomatic and economic relations with it. But that demand appears to have been dropped as a condition for the deal, according to US media reports. Trump has worked to cultivate a closer relationship with Saudi Arabia during his time in the White House. Along with the strategic and commercial gains likely to come with this deal, one possible reason for this agreement happening now is Russia and China have reportedly been in discussions on something similar and the US might not have wanted to miss out. The deal marks a major victory for Saudi Arabia, and it has the potential to reshape power dynamics in the Middle East - though not immediately and perhaps not for several years, if not longer. The nuclear issue has been at the centre of the months-long US-Israeli conflict with Iran. The two nations say one of the reasons they attacked Iran in February was to prevent the country from ever developing a nuclear weapon - something that Tehran denies planning to do. Saudi Arabia's de facto ruler, Crown Prince Mohammed bin Salman, told CBS News in 2018 that his own nation was not planning to acquire a nuclear weapon, but that "without a doubt if Iran developed a nuclear bomb, we will follow suit as soon as possible".

left.title: 'Laughably weak': GOP's criminal referral against Jack Smith dismissed as 'meaningless'

left-center.title: House Judiciary issues criminal referral against Jack Smith over alleged perjury

center.title: Jack Smith Faces DOJ Criminal Referral, Accused of Lying in Testimony

right-center.title: Jack Smith hit with criminal referral as GOP moves testimony fight toward prosecution

farLeftText: The House Judiciary Committee is referring former Special Counsel Jack Smith for criminal prosecution, but analysts are dismissing it. On Wednesday, Rep. Jim Jordan (R-OH) alleged that Smith "knowingly made false statements" and referred him to the Department of Justice for prosecution. Jordan, the chairman of the House Judiciary Committee, alleged in a letter to Acting Attorney General Todd Blanche that "strong evidence" appears to show Smith lied during a December 17 testimony. Smith is the prosecutor who brought two now-dismissed criminal cases against Trump relating to 2020 election interference and the retention of classified documents at Mar-a-Lago. The December 17 testimony mentioned in Jordan's letter refers to a closed-door House Judiciary deposition where Smith defended prosecuting Trump. Despite Jordan's strongly worded criminal referral against Smith, legal analysts, journalists, and Democratic lawmakers are dismissing it. Rep. Jamie Raskin (D-MD), the ranking member of the House Judiciary Committee, slammed Jordan's criminal referral in a public statement. Raskin was particularly upset with Jordan for faulting Smith for not volunteering National Archives and Records Administration presidential information that Republicans never asked about. "The Republicans fault Mr. Smith for not reading the minds of congressional investigators and for not volunteering information they didn't ask for," Raskin wrote. "This is absurd. Mr. Smith may be telegenic but he is not telepathic." The House Judiciary Committee Democrats' X account similarly blasted the referral as "laughably weak" and "vindictive" in a post. CNN political analyst Xochitl Hinojosa noted, "Trump called for Jack Smith's prosecution, so DOJ spent 20 months trying to find ANYTHING on Jack and failed," she wrote in a post on X. "Now we're seeing a desperate attempt to try to prosecute this longtime career prosecutor for following the facts and the law." Around the time news of the referral came out, Politico legal reporter Kyle Cheney simply reposted what he wrote in 2025: "Periodic reminder that criminal referrals from Congress, other than for contempt, are substantively meaningless."

centerText: House Judiciary Committee Chairman Jim Jordan has referred former special counsel Jack Smith to the Department of Justice (DOJ) for potential prosecution, accusing Smith of making false statements during his testimony before Congress. In a letter to acting Attorney General Todd Blanche on Wednesday, Jordan, a Republican, argued that newly released DOJ records contradict Smith's previous assertions regarding his office's access to communications involving members of Congress during the former DOJ special counsel's investigations of President Donald Trump. Jordan asked the DOJ to evaluate whether Smith's testimony violated federal laws governing false statements. Read More on News Newsweek reached out to the DOJ and Smith's lawyers via email Wednesday night for comment. ... What To Know The referral follows Senate Judiciary Committee Chairman Chuck Grassley, also a Republican, alleging to have records that he says show Smith's investigative team accessed text messages involving 44 members of Congress, including himself. "I received records frm [sic] DOJ confirming Jack Smith's investigative team reviewed the contents of text msgs sent by 44 MEMBERS OF CONGRESS Im 1 of the 44 Im alerting my colleagues who were impacted and will release the records w Sen Johnson so American ppl can see the evidence," Grassley said on X earlier this month. Jordan says that according to those records, investigators allegedly reviewed some communications before they underwent a standard "filter team" review designed to protect privileged material. Republicans contend those documents conflict with Smith's testimony that "gave the impression" neither he nor his team had access to lawmakers' privileged text messages. "This referral deliberately distorts and misstates the facts, including what questions the Republicans actually asked at Mr. Smith's interview. The Republicans fault Mr. Smith for not reading the minds of congressional investigators and for not volunteering information they didn't ask for. This is absurd. Mr. Smith may be telegenic but he is not telepathic," Democrat Jamie Raskin, ranking member on the House Judiciary Committee, said in a statement, according to Fox News. Is a DOJ Referral a Criminal Charge? A congressional criminal referral does not result in charges by itself. Instead, it asks the DOJ to determine whether an investigation or prosecution is warranted. It remains unclear whether the DOJ will pursue the allegations against Smith, who has consistently defended his work as special counsel and maintained that his investigations into Trump were conducted in accordance with the law. Former Palm Beach County State Attorney Dave Aronberg told Newsweek on Wednesday night that "This is performative politics masquerading as legal oversight, and it carries no direct legal weight. A congressional referral is simply a partisan suggestion, not a criminal charge. The Justice Department could open an inquiry to appease the President, but this is not a legitimate criminal matter. Instead, it’s a transparent attempt to misuse House committee oversight for political retaliation." Read Jordan's Letter to Todd Blanche PDF 2026-07-22-jdj-to-doj-blanche-re-smith-referral.pdf . PDF Download This is a developing story that will be updated with additional information.
centerRightText: NEW You can now listen to Fox News articles! FIRST ON FOX: Former Biden special counsel Jack Smith could face potential criminal prosecution after Republicans accused the ex-prosecutor of misleading Congress about the scope of his investigation into President Donald Trump. House Judiciary Committee Chairman Jim Jordan, R-Ohio, issued a criminal referral for Smith Wednesday, arguing newly released records contradict Smith's testimony to his committee that he did not obtain or review lawmakers' text messages during his multiple probes into President Donald Trump. "All individuals have an obligation to comply with a duly authorized congressional inquiry. Mr. Smith, a former public servant, is no different," Jordan wrote in a letter to acting Attorney General Todd Blanche on Wednesday. "Yet his testimony included intentionally false statements designed to obstruct the Committee’s oversight. "His conduct can only be understood as an effort to thwart the Committee’s inquiry by intentionally making false statements and representations before Congress," Jordan continued, adding that the Justice Department should examine whether Smith's actions warrant criminal charges. VETERAN DEM BREAKS PARTY LINE AS JACK SMITH SPYING SCANDAL GRIPS CAPITOL HILL Jordan’s letter accused Smith of making false statements during a December deposition before the House Judiciary Committee. Specifically, Jordan argued that "responses provided by Mr. Smith gave the impression that neither Mr. Smith nor his team had access to the content of members’ privileged text messages." "We now know this to be false," the letter adds. The referral comes after Smith faced mounting scrutiny from Republicans over allegedly weaponizing the Justice Department and violating constitutional privacy protections during his investigations into alleged interference during the 2020 election and Trump’s handling of classified documents. He has denied any wrongdoing. And Rep. Jamie Raskin, D-Md., the top Democrat on the House Judiciary Committee, blasted Jordan's referral. "This referral deliberately distorts and misstates the facts, including what questions the Republicans actually asked at Mr. Smith's interview. The Republicans fault Mr. Smith for not reading the minds of congressional investigators and for not volunteering information they didn't ask for. This is absurd. Mr. Smith may be telegenic but he is not telepathic," Raskin said in a statement. Senate Judiciary Committee Chairman Chuck Grassley, R-Iowa, released documents last week showing Smith's team read the text messages of 44 members of Congress from both parties and White House staff during its investigation. Targeted lawmakers included Jordan and Grassley in addition to the late Sen. Lindsey Graham, R-S.C.; Sen. Susan Collins, R-Maine; House Majority Leader Steve Scalise, R-La.; and Sen. Cory Booker, D-N.J. Trump officials affected included former Vice President Mike Pence, White House deputy chief of staff Stephen Miller, FBI Director Kash Patel and Ivanka Trump, among others in the president's orbit during his first term. BIDEN SPECIAL COUNSEL’S 'RUNAWAY TRAIN’ SCOOPED UP SENSITIVE LAWMAKER INFO: 'ABUSE OF POWER' Grassley's office also found that Smith's team appeared to violate investigative protocols by accessing text messages that had not been reviewed by a "filter team," a safeguard designed to prevent investigators from accessing privileged information, according to the documents. When Smith was asked by congressional investigators if he examined the contents of lawmakers’ text messages during his probe, he said, "no." He told the House Judiciary Committee his team only sought telephone toll records. "Toll records were sought for historical telephone routing information, collected after calls had taken place, identifying the incoming and outgoing call numbers, the time of the calls and their duration," Smith testified in December. "Toll records do not include the content of calls." CLICK HERE TO DOWNLOAD THE FOX NEWS APP The Justice Department told Fox News Digital when reached for comment, "The Department can confirm receipt of the criminal referral from the House Judiciary Committee and will investigate all evidence of criminal conduct."

topic: Mamdani Arrest Netanyahu Enforce Warrant Mayor

left.title: Mamdani says he can’t arrest Netanyahu as mayor and urges US to enforce ICC warrant

left-center.title: Mamdani: NYC Doesn’t Have ‘Legal Authority’ To Enforce ICC Arrest Warrant Against Netanyahu

farLeftText: Zohran Mamdani, the New York City mayor, doubled down on his support for the international criminal court (ICC) on Wednesday and called on federal authorities to execute the court’s warrant for Benjamin Netanyahu’s arrest should he visit the city in the fall. “I call on the federal government to join the international criminal court and to execute both this warrant as well as any warrant the international criminal court holds,” Mamdani told reporters at a press conference on Wednesday. Those remarks came less than 24 hours after Mamdani called the Israeli prime minister a “war criminal” who is “not welcome in New York City” in a video statement posted on social media. In the statement, the mayor acknowledged he had no legal authority to direct New York police to arrest Netanyahu should he attend the United Nations general assembly in September. Mamdani had previously said he was “in conversation” with city lawyers to determine whether his administration could arrest Netanyahu, as he had pledged to do during his mayoral campaign last year. On Wednesday, Mamdani rejected arguments from critics that Israel’s war crimes in Gaza have nothing to do with New York City. Israel’s actions in Gaza, he said, had “offended the conscience, the morals, the mere humanity of so many New Yorkers”. Mamdani’s statements about Netanyahu were remarkable for a US municipal leader and a signal of how support for Palestinian rights remains a signature policy for him. His insistence that the US government arrest Netanyahu has been met with condemnation and ridicule from pro-Israel officials. Pro-Palestinian advocates’ reactions were more mixed – some praised the mayor’s Tuesday statement for its “moral clarity” while others expressed disappointment that he walked back on his promise to seek the Israeli leader’s arrest. “I think the mayor should stay in his lane,” Jonathan Harounoff, Israel’s former international spokesperson at the United Nations, told NY1, accusing him of “political theater”. John Fetterman of Pennsylvania, one of the most staunchly pro-Israel Democrats in the Senate, called Mamdani a “clown” who should “sit down”. “America’s not even part of that corrupt court,” Fetterman said in an interview with Fox News over the weekend. “That’s really not your purview.” Simcha Eichenstein, a pro-Israel New York state assemblymember, had previously called on the state attorney general to review whether Mamdani’s comments about Netanyahu violated state law. The ICC issued arrest warrants against Netanyahu and the former Israeli defense minister Yoav Gallant in 2024, accusing them of war crimes and crimes against humanity, including “starvation as a method of warfare, and … murder, persecution, and other inhumane acts”. “I believe Prime Minister Netanyahu belongs in The Hague,” Mamdani told the New York Times last week, referring to the city in the Netherlands that is home to the court. Neither the US nor Israel are members of the ICC, which the US government has long opposed and which Marco Rubio, the secretary of state, this month threatened to “dismantle” by pressuring other countries to abandon it. Mamdani said he concluded his administration had no authority to execute the ICC arrest warrant, but shifted the burden on the Trump administration to do so. skip past newsletter promotion Free newsletter | Around 2-3 times a day Sign up to Breaking News US Get the most important news as it breaks Preview latest Enter your email Sign up after newsletter promotion “It is clear that we do not have the independent legal authority to enforce this warrant,” he said in Tuesday’s statement. “The federal government, however, does – and I call on them to join the ICC and execute this warrant.” Donald Trump has already said he will do no such thing. Netanyahu “will not be arrested” on US soil, the president said in a social media post earlier this week, adding that the Israeli prime minister was “fighting against the Islamic Republic of Iran” and “the only ones that should be arrested are the people that led Iran into this unprecedented spiral of death and destruction”. Netanyahu had said in the past he was “not concerned” with Mamdani’s threat, and his office said in a statement last week that the mayor “appears interested in diverting public attention from his follies and attacking the leader of the Jewish state and the only democracy in the Middle East”. It called the ICC a “kangaroo court”. Supporters of the court have also praised Mamdani’s efforts to stand up for the tribunal’s mission. “My mayor is doing what any NYC mayor would do if Vladimir Putin or any other indicted war criminal planned to visit NYC,” said Adil Haque, a legal scholar. “He is exploring every legal avenue and exhausting all legal options. If his hands are tied, he will use his voice.”
centerLeftText: LOADING ERROR LOADING Mayor Zohran Mamdani on Tuesday acknowledged the limitations of New York City’s legal authority when it comes to enforcing an arrest warrant against Israeli Prime Minister Benjamin Netanyahu, but noted that the federal government doesn’t face the same constraints. “My administration has reviewed every avenue available under applicable law to determine whether New York City can execute the International Criminal Court’s arrest warrant if Benjamin Netanyahu came here. It is clear that we do not have the independent legal authority to enforce this warrant,” Mamdani said in a video posted to X. “The federal government, however, does, and I call on them to join the ICC and execute this warrant.” Advertisement During his mayoral campaign, Mamdani pledged to order Netanyahu’s arrest, and he recently told The New York Times that he was in active discussions with his office’s legal department about what the law would allow him to do. In the wake of that interview, President Donald Trump vowed that the Israeli leader, who could visit New York City this fall for the U.N. General Assembly, would not be arrested in the U.S. in “any way, shape, or form.” Benjamin Netanyahu is a war criminal. pic.twitter.com/YRezmW6YVx — Mayor Zohran Kwame Mamdani (@NYCMayor) July 22, 2026 Advertisement In 2024, the ICC issued an arrest warrant for Netanyahu and accused him of crimes against humanity, which he denied. That warrant stated that Netanyahu was “allegedly responsible for the war crimes of starvation as a method of warfare and of intentionally directing an attack against the civilian population” as part of Israel’s military campaign against Gaza. The U.S. is not a party to the Rome Statute, which established the ICC, and does not observe its authority. The Trump administration has also taken a more hostile posture toward the ICC, with Secretary of State Marco Rubio recently launching a campaign aimed at dismantling the body. Mamdani, who described Netanyahu as “the architect of a horrific genocide against the Palestinian people,” also detailed why the prime minister should be arrested. For the targeting of neonatal hospitals and maternity care centers, denying newborns even the chance to live. For the countless people he starved as he blocked food and humanitarian aid from reaching them. For the gunning down of hundreds of aid workers and journalists,” Mamdani alleged in his video on Tuesday. “And just last month, the U.N. confirmed that Palestinian children continue to be deliberately targeted and killed by Israeli armed forces more than eight months after the so-called ceasefire.” “I agree with the ICC that Benjamin Netanyahu should be arrested and tried for his crimes, as I do for anyone else charged by the ICC,” Mamdani added. In a statement on Sunday, Netanyahu’s office attacked the ICC, calling it a “kangaroo court,” and criticized Mamdani in response. It also claimed that Israel had “taken unprecedented wartime measures to minimize harm to civilians while confronting Hamas.”

topic: Trump Attends Dignified Transfer Soldiers Killed

left-center.title: Trump attends dignified transfer of soldiers killed in Iranian attacks

right-center.title: Trump salutes 'great heroes' as he attends dignified transfer of US service members killed in Jordan, Iraq

right.title: President Trump Attends Dignified Transfer of Fallen American Soldiers

farLeftText: No article found in this category for this topic.
centerLeftText: Washington — President Trump and Defense Secretary Pete Hegseth attended a dignified transfer at Dover Air Force Base in Delaware on Wednesday honoring the four U.S. Army soldiers killed in the Middle East in recent days during the ongoing war with Iran. The Trump administration is investigating attacks at Muwaffaq Salti Air Base in Jordan that led to the deaths of two of the soldiers, and the presumed death of one other who was initially described as missing. The dignified transfer also included an Army sergeant killed at a base in northern Iraq during a controlled detonation of an Iranian drone. The service members who were killed were: Lt. Tyler James Feehan, 25, of Hawaii Pvt. Isabella Gonzales, 19, of Texas Sgt. Angel Rampersad, 28, of New York Sgt. Michael Emmanuel Swinton, 30, of North Carolina The White House said the president met with each of the soldiers' families. President Trump and Defense Secretary Pete Hegseth salute during a dignified transfer for Sgt. Angel S. Rampersad at Dover Air Force Base in Dover, Delaware, on July 22, 2026. SAUL LOEB /AFP via Getty Images "We're going to Dover," the president told reporters before attending the transfer. "Some of you are going with us, to honor our heroes, and they are indeed great heroes, actually. They said, and they — all of them said very strongly — we can't let, we cannot let Iran have a nuclear weapon. They won't have a nuclear weapon. So we're going to honor them. And it's one of the hardest things to do. For me, it's one of the hardest things to do as a president. But, has to be done." Feehan and Gonzales were both killed in action during the strikes on the base in Jordan, according to the Pentagon. U.S. Central Command had announced Rampersad as missing in action, but updated her status Tuesday to whereabouts unknown, and she is believed to have been killed in the same attack. Swinton, a father of two, was killed at Erbil Air Base in northern Iraq during the detonation of an Iranian drone. From left: Pvt. Isabella Gonzales, 19, of Carrollton, Texas; Sgt. Angel Rampersad, 28, of Ozone Park, New York; 1st Lt. Tyler James Feehan, 25, of Ewa Beach, Hawaii; and Sgt. Michael Swinton, 30, of Fayetteville, North Carolina. Department of Defense The president told reporters Wednesday that the administration would share the results of the probe into the attacks in Jordan. A dignified transfer is a nonceremonial movement of a fallen U.S. service member killed in a theater abroad back to the United States. The fallen are moved in flag-draped cases, not caskets, and carried by members of the military. It is customary for the president or other high-ranking administration officials to attend.

President Donald Trump on Wednesday saluted the flag-draped caskets of four U.S. service members recently killed in action in Jordan and Iraq while attended a dignified transfer ceremony at Dover Air Force Base in Delaware.

Trump stood alongside Secretary of War Pete Hegseth as they paid tribute to 1st Lt. Tyler James Feehan, 25, of Ewa Beach, Hawaii; Sgt. Angel Rampersad, 28, of Ozone Park, New York; and Pvt. Isabella Gonzales, 19, of Carrollton, Texas, who were killed in Jordan, and Sgt. Michael Emmanuel Swinton, 30, from Fayetteville, North Carolina, who was killed in Iraq.

Trump told reporters ahead of the ceremony the service members were "great heroes."

"We're going to honor them. And it’s one of the hardest things to do — for me, it's one of the hardest things to do as a president. But it has to be done," Trump added.

US SOLDIER KILLED IN JORDAN ATTACK REMEMBERED AS LIVING ‘LIFE OF COURAGE AND SELFLESSNESS’

"All I'm going to say is we love you. We love your child," Trump said when asked if he had a message for the families. "And that's what they are to them. They're their children. There's no games, no nothing. That's their child. And all you could do is throw out your heart."

Feehan’s parents, Stephen and Shari, told KUTV Tuesday, "No parents could have asked for a better son, and carrying your legacy forward is now our purpose.

"To that end, we're building a scholarship in his name — one meant to open doors to education for those who serve or hope to. We're starting it with his death gratuity, and we welcome anyone moved to give."

RUBIO ACCUSES IRAN OF FLYING IRGC PERSONNEL INTO YEMEN AS HOUTHI THREAT ESCALATES

U.S. Central Command said over the weekend that the service members were killed at Muwaffaq Salti Air Base in Jordan as CENTCOM and partner forces "defended against Iranian ballistic missile and drone attacks."

Swinton was killed while troops attempted to destroy a downed Iranian attack drone Sunday at an air base in Iraq.

Both incidents remain under investigation.

farRightText:
President Donald Trump attended on Wednesday the dignified transfer of the four servicemembers killed in the Middle East from Friday into the weekend.

The president and other distinguished visitors observed the dignified transfer of 1st Lt. Tyler James Feehan, 25; Sgt. Angel S. Rampersad, 28; Sgt. Michael Emmanuel Swinton, 30; and Pvt. Isabella Gonzales, 19, at Dover Air Force Base in Delaware.

Feehan and Gonzales were killed in action following Iran’s ballistic missile and drone attack on Muwaffaq Salti Air Base in Jordan on Friday. Rampersad, who was announced as missing after the attack, is believed to have been killed during the assault, according to the Department of War. Swinton was killed on Sunday during the controlled detonation of a downed one-way attack drone at Erbil Air Base in Iraq.

All incidents are under investigation.

Feehan hails from Ewa Beach, Hawaii; Gonzales from Carrollton, Texas; Swinton from Fayetteville, North Carolina; and Rampersad from Ozone Park, New York.

On his way to the Air Base, Trump said receiving the remains of America’s fallen is “one of the hardest things to do as a president.”

Upon arriving at Dover Air Base, Trump met with the families of the fallen before the dignified transfer, according to the White House press pool.

The president, standing next to Secretary of War Pete Hegseth, saluted each transfer case as it was transported from the C-17 to a vehicle on the Air Base.

Chairman of the Joint Chiefs of Staff Dan Caine and, according to the White House press pool, a dignitary who appeared to be acting Chief of Staff of the U.S. Army Gen. Christopher LaNeve, Chief of Naval Operations Adm. Daryl Caudle, White House press secretary Karoline Leavitt, and executive assistant to the president Natalie Harp were on hand for the transfer.

The president departed for Georgia after the event, and Feehan’s family traveled aboard Air Force One with him.

“The family of LT Tyler Feehan will be flying to Georgia with us. They are from Georgia, and the president offered to give them a ride home,” a White House official said.

On Monday, Trump pledged that Iran will pay “many times over” for each American service member it kills.

“Every time Iran kills an American Soldier they will pay for that killing many times over!” Trump wrote in a post on Truth Social. “This directive has been passed on to Secretary of War, Pete Hegseth, Chairman of the Joint Chiefs of Staff, Daniel Caine, and every Leader in the Military.”

topic: Police Madison Wisconsin Sparking Protests Officer

left.title: Police officer fatally shoots man in Madison, Wisconsin, sparking protests

left-center.title: Man shot and killed by police in Madison, Wisconsin, sparking protests

farLeftText: A police officer in Madison, Wisconsin, shot and killed a man Wednesday in an incident that was captured on cellphone footage, sparking local protests. John Patterson, the Madison police chief, acknowledged the shooting at a news conference, but stressed footage circulating was just one perspective of what happened. “I want to take a moment to honestly and sincerely offer my heartfelt sympathies to his family for this loss,” Patterson said. The dead man was in his 30s, Patterson said, but police did not release his name. The shooting happened on a city street in a popular neighborhood lined with restaurants, bars, shops and residential homes less than 2 miles from the state capitol. Footage of the incident shows numerous cars stopped in the intersection with people watching as it unfolded. “Another Black man is dead on a Madison street, and our community watched him die before we even knew his name,” said Brandi Grayson, the head of Urban Triage, a Madison-based Black community advocacy group. “That is the cruelty of state violence.” A crowd formed near where the shooting occurred on Wednesday with people chanting: “Arrest the cops!” Police had been responding to calls that someone was attempting to enter parked vehicles in the neighborhood, Patterson said. The suspect fled on a bike through nearby backyards in an attempt to get away before officers contacted him in the street, Patterson said. The suspect either fell off his bike or was taken off his bike by officers and during the struggle pulled out a knife and injured an officer, Patterson said. In one of the videos circulating online, three officers are seen holding the man down and attempting to arrest him. The officers lose control of the suspect, who frees his arms and attempts to get up off his knees. One officer appears to reach for his pistol, when a female officer runs toward the others shouting: “Taser! Taser!” That officer did not draw his firearm. After some jostling, the suspect falls to the ground. As he goes down, another officer rises to his feet, pulls out his pistol, leans down toward the man on the ground and fires three shots at close range. The officer who fired the shots then re-holsters his gun and places the suspect’s arm behind his back, before standing up and walking away. Two other officers push the suspect over onto his belly, before others arrive to attempt to offer medical attention. The officer who fired the lethal shots had been injured by the suspect with a knife, Patterson said. He described the officer as a veteran of the police force, but didn’t provide his name. Another officer was injured, but it’s not clear how, Patterson said. The other two officers were uninjured. The race of the officer who fired his gun on Wednesday and that of the man killed were not released by police. But advocates for the Black community in Madison said the man killed was Black. Sabrina Madison, the city council president, called the videos “both disturbing and painful”, in a statement. “I’ve received several calls from alders with questions and what I can say without a doubt is that I expect a full, comprehensive, and transparent independent investigation into today’s shooting of a community member,” the statement reads. The Wisconsin department of justice criminal investigation will investigate the shooting because it involves police officers. A white police officer shot and killed Tony Robinson, who was biracial, on the same street in 2015, a shooting that spurred large protests. The district attorney did not file charges against police in that incident, determining the officer had been justified. The Associated Press contributed
centerLeftText: A Madison police officer shot and killed a man who had injured him with a knife while resisting arrest, the city's police chief said Wednesday. Police have not identified the man who was killed, saying only that he was in his 30s. "I want to take a moment to honestly and sincerely offer my heartfelt sympathies to his family for this loss," Madison Police Chief John Patterson said at a news conference Wednesday. Cellphone video of the shooting circulated on social media shortly after the incident. Three gunshots can be heard on the cellphone video. Patterson stressed that the footage circulating was just one perspective of what happened. "I ask the community to recognize that there is other video that they have not seen, that's not circulating online, that will be reviewed as part of this investigation," he said. "So, to the extent that they can as we all navigate this trauma together, we try to remain calm and remember that not all perspectives are online right now." Madison Police Chief John Patterson speaking at a news conference after a Madison police officer shot and killed a man in Madison, Wis., Wednesday, July 22, 2026. WKOW via AP The shooting happened on a city street in a popular neighborhood lined with restaurants, bars, shops and residential homes less than two miles from the state Capitol. Footage of the incident shows numerous cars stopped in the intersection with people watching as it unfolded. A white police officer shot and killed Tony Robinson, who was biracial, on the same street in 2015, a shooting that spurred large protests. The district attorney did not file charges against police in that incident, determining the officer was justified. His family accepted a $3.35 million settlement from the city in 2017. The race of the officer who fired his gun on Wednesday and that of the man killed were not released by police. But advocates for the Black community in Madison said the man killed was Black. "Another Black man is dead on a Madison street, and our community watched him die before we even knew his name," said Brandi Grayson, the head of the Madison-based Black community advocacy group Urban Triage. "That is the cruelty of state violence." Gubernatorial candidate Francesca Hong speaks alongside protesters near where a Madison police officer shot and killed a man Wednesday, July 22, 2026, in Madison, Wis. AP Photo/Kayla Wolf A crowd formed near where the shooting occurred on Wednesday, with some people chanting, "Arrest the cops!" Among the protesters who gathered throughout the night was Wisconsin gubernatorial candidate Francesca Hong. "We're gonna make sure that we're able to support any assembly that might happen, any protest that might happen, but also not wanting to amplify any issues or create friction where it's unnecessary, frankly, so we'll be reaching out to community members and community leaders to hopefully strike the right balance," Patterson said. Wisconsin Gov. Tony Evers said in a social media post he was "briefed on the officer-involved shooting that occurred in Madison today" and continues "to receive updates as more information is released." "There must be transparency and accountability in any investigation moving forward," Evers said in the post. Police were responding to calls that someone was attempting to enter parked vehicles in the neighborhood, Patterson said. The suspect fled on a bike through nearby backyards in an attempt to get away before officers contacted him in the street, the police chief said. The suspect either fell off his bike or was taken off his bike by officers, and during the struggle pulled out a knife and injured an officer, Patterson said. An officer deployed a taser but it was unsuccessful, the chief said. The same officer who was injured by the knife fired his weapon and the man who was shot later died at the hospital, the police chief said. He described the officer who fired his weapon as a veteran officer, but did not reveal any other details. "When a knife is produced during officers' apprehension arrest, the overall situation is clearly elevated. It becomes incredibly dangerous and it obviously increases risks for everyone there," Patterson said. "For every officer. For the person as well. I think it severely narrows the scope of options and possible outcomes." "This was not a small, folding knife. It's a large, fixed-blade knife," Patterson added, telling reporters a photo of the knife would eventually be shared. "My understanding is there are some witnesses that also saw this, so I think we gotta, as a community, recognize we don't have all the information right now, and we have to allow our investigators to piece things together and get as clear a picture as we can," he said. Another officer was injured, but it's not clear how, Patterson said, noting police don't believe that officer's injuries were caused by the knife. The other two officers were uninjured. All four officers will be placed on administrative leave, as is standard protocol for the department, during the investigation. The Wisconsin Department of Criminal Investigation will investigate the shooting because it involves police officers.

topic: Houthis Claim Tankers Strikes Launches Iran

left.title: Yemen’s Houthis claim attacks on Red Sea tankers as US launches 12th night of strikes on Iran – Middle East crisis live

left-center.title: Houthis Claim Strikes on 2 Saudi Oil Tankers in Red Sea

center.title: Houthis claim attack on oil tankers as US launches more strikes on Iran

farLeftText: From 25m ago 02.00 EDT Interim summary In case you’re just joining us, here’s a snapshot of the latest developments in the Middle East as the threat of a new front opens up in the US-Iran war. It’s 9.30am in Tehran and 2am in Washington. Yemen’s ⁠Iran-backed Houthi militia said it attacked two ​Saudi Arabian oil tankers in the Red Sea , with a Saudi news agency later confirming one of the vessels was ablaze on Thursday. The strikes came after the Houthis declared a maritime blockade of Saudi Arabia – a move that current and former US officials said could significantly widen the US-Iran war and strain the American military. The US launched its 12th successive night of strikes against Iran , saying military targets were hit to degrade Tehran’s ability to attack commercial vessels. Iranian state TV said two people were killed and 11 wounded in what a Khuzestan provincial official described as a US missile attack on the Shalamcheh border crossing with Iraq. Iranian media said the US twice attacked a military target in Bushehr, near the country’s only operating commercial nuclear power reactor, marking three strikes there in two days. Scattered debris and smoke at an unknown location during what the US military said were strikes on Iran. Photograph: US Central Command/Reuters Iran’s Islamic Revolutionary Guard Corps (IRGC) said it attacked US forces in Kuwait’s Al-Adiri camp and Ali Al Salem airbase, while the army said its attacks there included on the Arifjan and Doha camps. Before the latest US strikes the Islamic republic’s foreign minister warned on social media: “Our defense doctrine is clear: eye for an eye.” Abbas Araghchi also said: “Any aggression against Iran, including our infrastructure, will compel a powerful and decisive response. Those who contribute to such aggression, whatever the kind of support, will also be considered as legitimate targets.” The US and Saudi Arabia signed a landmark nuclear agreement that could allow the kingdom to enrich uranium in the future. The deal has caused alarm across the Middle East and in Washington. Its full details are yet to be released but, as Patrick Wintour reports, the timing of the announcement is controversial as the US’s war with Iran is being fought in part to prevent Tehran enriching uranium domestically and make it near-impossible for Iran to build a nuclear weapon. The IRGC said on Thursday it stopped three oil tankers from passing through the strait of Hormuz . It said in a statement quoted by Iranian state media on Thursday that the three oil tankers “provoked” by the US military were trying to pass through a mine-laden route south of the strait “but after one of them exploded and caught fire, the other two quickly turned around and turned back”. Earlier, Donald Trump warned on Truth Social that from now any time Iran shot at a ship in the Hormuz strait, the US “ will bomb and destroy ONE BRIDGE OR POWER PLANT, including those located next to, or in, the Capital City of Tehran””. The IRGC said its navy “emphasises that the strait of Hormuz is under our control”. US Central Command posted on X: “Iran does not control the Strait of Hormuz. The international waterway remains open for transit regardless of IRGC threats and attacks.” Two very large Chinese crude carriers carrying a combined 4m barrels of Saudi Arabian oil are heading towards the Bab ⁠el-Mandeb strait to exit the ⁠Red Sea, ​shipping data showed on Thursday – a test of how tightly the Houthis will impose their ⁠naval blockade on Saudi Arabia. Share Updated at 02.24 EDT 39m ago 01.47 EDT Asean summit to call for open straits amid Iran war Foreign ministers from fuel-stressed south-east Asia will call for freedom of navigation in international straits when they wrap a week of meetings largely overshadowed by the US-Iran war, according to a draft communique seen by Agence France-Presse. It reports that the draft statement expected to emerge from the Association of Southeast Asian Nations (Asean) gathering in Manila will also repeat calls for self-restraint in a Middle East conflict that has carried economic ripple effects throughout the region. Visiting US secretary of state Marco Rubio this week warned Asean envoys that Iranian control of the strait of Hormuz would set a “dangerous precedent”, saying: “There is a fundamental principle about freedom of navigation that is threatened.” View image in fullscreen Foreign ministers at the Asean forum in Metro Manila on Thursday. Photograph: Ezra Acayan/Getty Images The draft communique inserts previously unused language targeting “freedom of navigation in and overflight above straits” while separately calling for “unimpeded” passage through the Hormuz strait. Tehran’s closure of the crucial energy waterway after US-Israeli attacks has left many in the region scrambling to deal with energy shortages and price spikes, with the import-dependent Philippines declaring a national emergency in March. But while the communique alludes to energy cooperation among Asean states, no progress on previously announced initiatives like a regional energy grid or fuel-sharing scheme has been revealed Share Updated at 01.53 EDT 1h ago 01.16 EDT US attacks against Iran on Thursday lasted five hours and struck at Iranian military targets including maritime capabilities, missile and ⁠drone storage facilities and air defence assets, according to the US military. Iranian media reports said the US twice on Thursday attacked a military target in Bushehr, near the country’s only operating commercial nuclear power reactor, marking three strikes there in two days. State TV reported two people were killed and 11 wounded in what a Khuzestan provincial official described as a US missile attack on the Shalamcheh border crossing with Iraq. There’s more in our latest full report: Houthis claim strikes on two Saudi tankers as US attacks on Iran widen Read more Share 2h ago 00.46 EDT Iran’s Revolutionary Guard is reportedly saying it had attacked US forces in Kuwait’s Al-Adiri camp and Ali Al Salem airbase. Iranian state media is meanwhile quoting the army as saying it has attacked US bases in Kuwait including the Arifjan and Doha camps. The reports come as Iran has continued to strike out at regional countries hosting US forces amid the ongoing American strikes against Iran. Share 2h ago 00.35 EDT More now on Iranian-aligned Houthis attacking two Saudi oil tankers earlier today. The Houthis identified the two vessels as the Encelia and the Layla. Saudi state news ​agency SPA cited an official source as saying the Encelia was struck, causing a fire at the bow. ‌The attack on the Layla remained unconfirmed. A maritime security source said ‌the Encelia had transmitted a distress call, reporting it had been struck by a missile near the Saudi port of Jizan in the Red Sea late on Wednesday. Millions of barrels a day of Saudi oil have been heading to the kingdom’s ‌Red Sea port of Yanbu to avoid the strait of Hormuz. In announcing their ‌attack on the two tankers, ​Yemen’s Houthis claimed they had forced about 10 ships to retreat and return. Five tankers changed course in the Red Sea on Wednesday, with two signalling the Suez canal as their new destination after the Houthis warned ships to avoid Saudi ports, ship-tracking data showed. centerText:
Yemen's Houthis say they have attacked two Saudi oil tankers in the Red Sea as the US launched its 12th consecutive night of strikes on Iran.

Iranian state media said two people had been killed in the US strikes on Wednesday, and a tanker caught fire in an explosion in a mined route in the Strait of Hormuz.

Ahead of the latest round of strikes, US President Donald Trump had threatened to destroy an Iranian bridge or power plant if Iran attacked a ship in the strait and to "take care of" the Houthis if they blocked the Red Sea.

If confirmed, the Houthi attack would be the first since the Iran-backed group announced a "maritime embargo" against Saudi Arabia.

The Houthis named the tankers as the Encelia and the Layla, though their responsibility has not been confirmed.

The group's military spokesperson Yahya Saree said they had targeted the ships with drones and missiles for allegedly violating its naval blockade.

Saree added that the Houthis promised to continue their naval operations and would continue "enforcing the 'siege for a siege' equation".

The state-run Saudi Press Agency confirmed early on Thursday that the Encelia was struck "while sailing in the Red Sea", resulting in a fire on its bow.

Citing an unnamed official source at the Transport General Authority, it described the attack as a violation of international law. All crew were safe, it reported.

UK Maritime Trade Operation (UKMTO) reported an "unknown projectile" struck a Saudi tanker about 130km from Al Shuqaiq in Saudi Arabia at 20:00 GMT. The attack caused a fire but no casualties, it said.

British maritime risk management group Vanguard also reported that the Saudi tanker Encelia had been hit.

The attack on the Layla has not been confirmed.

US Central Command (Centcom) said at 21:30 GMT it had launched strikes at Trump's direction "to further degrade Iran's ability to threaten civilian mariners and commercial vessels transiting regional waters".

The strikes targeted "Iranian military targets including maritime capabilities, missile and drone storage facilities, coastal surveillance sites, and air defense assets," it said.

Kuwait, Jordan and Bahrain soon reported responding to airborne threats, before Iran's military said they had targeted US assets in all three countries, according to AFP.

Iranian state media reported two people had been killed in US strikes on the country's south near its border with Iraq.

Meanwhile, the Iranian Revolutionary Guard (IRGC) said the Strait of Hormuz was "completely closed" after an explosion in the channel, Iranian state media reported.

The IRGC said it had stopped three oil tankers from passing through the strait.

One tanker caught fire and two others turned back after attempting to pass through a mined route south of the strait, according to Iranian state media.

It comes after Trump promised earlier on Wednesday to attack an Iranian bridge or power plant if its forces hit ships in the Strait of Hormuz.

Iran's Foreign Minister Abbas Araghchi later warned of an "eye for an eye" response to attacks on Iran.

On Tuesday, Trump told reporters he would "take care of the Houthis" should they obstruct the Red Sea as they had said.

Few details of the "maritime embargo" were given by the Houthis when it was announced on Monday, but a media official said they were closing the Bab al-Mandab Strait - the Red Sea's southern gateway - to Saudi vessels.

center.title: News Wrap: Tropical Storm Bertha makes landfall in Louisiana

farLeftText: No article found in this category for this topic.
centerLeftText: Tropical Storm Bertha made landfall in St. Bernard Parish, Louisiana, on Wednesday. The storm is bringing rain to a large swath of the northern Gulf Coast, with a series of watches and warnings issued along its path. Bertha is crawling westward "with little acceleration," the National Hurricane Center said. Forecasters said the storm would move over the New Orleans area overnight Wednesday and continue on toward Houston before dissipating by the end of the week. Beaches were closed from Florida to Mississippi as Bertha's tropical-storm-force winds reached more than 100 miles outward from its center. Map shows projected path Bertha, the second named storm of the Atlantic hurricane season, is forecast to stick close to the coast. The Miami-based hurricane center said it expects the system to continue gradually weakening as it moves along the northern Gulf Coast over the next couple of days. As of the center's latest update early Thursday, the storm had maximum sustained winds of 45 mph after gradually decreasing since Tuesday night. CBS News Louisiana Gov. Jeff Landry urged residents not to be complacent about the potential flooding threat. "It doesn't take a major hurricane to do major damage to communities in Louisiana," Landry said at a news conference Wednesday. Louisiana state climatologist Jay Grymes said he expected some pockets of flooding, but added, "With luck, this will be an in-and-out kind of situation." Robbie Berg, a warning coordination meteorologist at the hurricane center, told The Associated Press that the storm's landfall itself shouldn't be too much of a concern. "Landfall really doesn't have a whole lot of meaning in this particular case," Berg said. Candice Henderson, who manages a beachside restaurant called the Crab Trap in Pensacola, told The AP that, for local residents, "this is just another storm." But officials in Florida, Louisiana and Mississippi still opened sites where people could acquire sandbags, while some beaches were closed to swimmers. Warnings and watches in effect Tropical storm watches were expanded Wednesday to include coastal parts of southwestern Louisiana and northeastern Texas as the storm traveled in their direction. Tropical storm warnings were in effect for west of Cameron, Louisiana, to Sargent, Texas, as well as the area of New Orleans and Lake Pontchartrain. A tropical storm watch was also posted for the area west of Cameron to Sargent, Texas. "Tropical storm conditions are possible within the watch area in Louisiana by tonight and along the upper Texas coast by Thursday or Thursday night," the hurricane center said Wednesday. The storm was expected to produce 1 to 4 inches of rain, with isolated totals of 6 inches in some areas through Thursday. "This rainfall may produce isolated flash flooding, especially in urban areas," the hurricane center warned. The system could also produce storm surge, raising waters up to 3 feet in some locations along the coast. Bertha comes weeks after the 2026 hurricane season's first named storm, Tropical Storm Arthur, which made landfall in June along the Gulf Coast of Texas.
centerText: In our news wrap Wednesday, Tropical Storm Bertha made landfall on the coast of Louisiana about 40 miles east of New Orleans, this year's cyclosporiasis outbreak in the U.S. is officially the worst on record and OpenAI says it's investigating an "unprecedented cyber incident" after one of its AI models went rogue and hacked another company. farLeftText: No article found in this category for this topic.

centerText: Austria has opened a police station in the house where Adolf Hitler was born in the town of Braunau am Inn. It follows years of controversy about what to do with the 17th-Century former inn, where Nazi dictator Adolf Hitler was born in April 1889. The building has now been substantially refurbished and its facade changed, but it has been a huge headache for Austrian authorities, who want to prevent it from becoming a site of neo-Nazi pilgrimage. Hitler only lived in Salzburger Vorstadt 15 for a few weeks before his family moved to another address in Braunau. They left the town for good when he was three years old. Hitler returned briefly to Braunau in 1938, on his way to Vienna, after he annexed Austria to Nazi Germany. The opening ceremony for the police station, featuring police musicians playing a tuba and other brass instruments, was attended by Interior Minister Gerhard Karner and other local dignitaries. Austria was taking "responsibility" for the "terrible legacy" of the past, said Karner. While deciding what to do with the building had not been easy, Karner believed the police station marked "a new chapter" for it. "This house will now become a place of democracy, rule of law, security, freedom and human dignity," he said. A Catholic police chaplain then blessed two crosses which will hang in the new police station. For decades, the interior ministry had rented the building from its former owner, a local woman called Gerlinde Pommer. It paid her a generous rent, in an attempt to prevent the house being used as a site for extreme right-wing tourism.
