centerRightText:
House Judiciary Committee Chairman Jim Jordan (R-OH) on Wednesday issued a criminal referral of former special counsel Jack Smith to the Department of Justice over allegations he lied to Congress about tracking personal phone messages of dozens of lawmakers.

Republicans have long raised concerns about Smith’s time at President Joe Biden’s DOJ. Smith obtained subpoenas that allowed him to access communications records of mostly Republican lawmakers as part of the DOJ’s and FBI’s “Arctic Frost” investigation. Last week, Senate Judiciary Committee Chairman Chuck Grassley (R-IA) released data he said indicated Smith’s team reviewed text messages from 44 senators and representatives from both parties, though mostly Republican.

During testimony before the House Judiciary Committee last December, Smith denied that he had examined the contents of lawmakers’ text messages. Jordan said in a letter this week to acting Attorney General Todd Blanche that he is submitting the criminal referral due to what he described as new evidence that Smith committed perjury. A DOJ official confirmed to the Washington Examiner that the department “will investigate all evidence of criminal conduct” in the referral.

Advertisement

“All individuals have an obligation to comply with a duly authorized congressional inquiry. Mr. Smith, a former public servant, is no different,” Jordan wrote in the letter, reported by Fox News. “Yet his testimony included intentionally false statements designed to obstruct the Committee’s oversight.” “While Mr. Smith testified that he did not seek a search warrant from telecommunications carriers for the content of Members’ text messages, he failed to disclose that his office received text message content from [the National Archives and Records Administration] relating to White House personnel phone records,” Jordan wrote, accusing Smith of having “excluded information that was highly relevant to the Committee’s examination.” During his House testimony last year, Smith claimed his team only sought telephone toll records. Advertisement “Toll records were sought for historical telephone routing information, collected after calls had taken place, identifying the incoming and outgoing call numbers, the time of the calls, and their duration,” he said. “Toll records do not include the content of calls.” Smith is known for leading the Jan. 6, 2021, election-interference and classified-documents cases against President Donald Trump. The Arctic Frost investigation was triggered by Smith’s prosecution of Trump due to alleged election interference in 2020 in the Jan. 6 case. Cruz has said Smith sent subpoenas to AT&T for his cellphone records, voicemails, text messages, and geolocation data. BILL PULTE IS URGED TO REVOKE SECURITY CLEARANCES FOR EX-JACK SMITH TEAM MEMBERS Advertisement Before saying last week that records prove Smith’s team reviewed personal text messages of 44 lawmakers, Grassley had said Smith issued 197 subpoenas to 34 individuals and 163 businesses, including financial institutions, as part of Arctic Frost, in conjunction with former FBI Director Christopher Wray. The subpoenas requested records and communications related to over 430 individuals and organizations, ”all of them appear to be aimed at Republicans,” according to Grassley. Cruz said last November that, in the investigation, U.S. District Judge James Boasberg signed off on subpoenas from Smith’s office that held “no basis,” ordering phone companies to allow the government to monitor the phone lines of nine Republican senators, including himself. Verizon complied with the subpoenas, while AT&T pushed back. “When you have the Biden Department of Justice subpoenaing 20% of the Republicans in the Senate, it is an egregious abuse of process,” Cruz said. “It is a fishing expedition, and we need to have a full and thorough investigation and accountability for every executive branch official who signed off on weaponizing the government.”

topic: Trump Economic Policies Georgia Scoffs Affordability

left.title: Trump scoffs at ‘affordability’ as he touts economic policies at Georgia rally

right.title: Trump Rallies in Georgia, Touting the Trump Accounts, New Policies, and Key Economic Wins

farLeftText: Donald Trump scoffed at the word “affordability” as a political message, describing it at a campaign rally on Wednesday as a term concocted by Democratic political consultants to criticize his presidency. Yet even as the president dismissed the notion of affordability, he devoted much of his speech at Wheeler high school to touting the bread-and-butter benefits of his economic policies. “So I just want you to remember that if you’re paying a little bit more – a lot of it’s come down now, we got it coming down very rapidly actually,” Trump said in Marietta, an Atlanta suburb, casting higher prices today as a lingering effect of the Biden administration. Americans are broadly disappointed with Trump’s handling of the economy, with just 33% of voters approving in a recent Washington Post-Ipsos poll, an assessment that could hurt his party’s hopes of bucking historical precedent and retaining control of Congress in November’s midterm elections. The president was in Georgia, a politically divided state where high-profile contests for governor and Senate are playing out, to promote his newly funded “Trump accounts”, which establish tax-free investment funds for every child born through 2028, seeded with $1,000 from the federal government. Speaking before the president, treasury secretary Scott Bessent described the accounts as the “triumph of capitalism over socialism”, taking a swipe at New York mayor Zohran Mamdani. “Look around our campuses and classrooms today – apart from those here at Wheeler, of course – you will find that our students are being sold the seductive lie that government can solve every problem, redistribute every dollar and supplant its judgment for our own,” Bessent said. “In New York, the current mayor rose to power by repackaging failed ideals of the past as a moral cause of the future. But in Georgia, you know that beneath that youthful veneer lies the oldest impulse in politics which is to concentrate power and then to call it compassion.” Several Republican candidates joined Trump in Marietta, where the president likened US senator Jon Ossoff, a Democrat seeking re-election in one of the most closely watched races of the cycle, to the character Pee-wee Herman, portrayed by the late actor Paul Reubens. Polling shows Ossoff with a lead over the Republican nominee in the Senate race, US congressman Mike Collins, who was in Washington where his vote was needed to help pass a budget bill to fund the Iran war and other White House priorities. Rick Jackson, Georgia’s Republican candidate for governor, pledged to ask the state legislature to match the $1,000 federal contribution to childhood Trump investment accounts if elected. If they failed to do so, Jackson said he would match the figure himself. Jackson is the founder of Jackson Healthcare, a medical staffing company. His personal net worth has been estimated to be more than $3bn. In 2024, the state of Georgia recorded more than 126,000 live births, which equates to $126m in annual contributions for Trump accounts. Georgia began the 2026 fiscal year with an unprecedented budget cushion of about $15bn in the bank, with $9bn in unrestricted funds. This surplus resulted from conservative budgeting that began in 2021 as a reaction to pandemic pressures. That money has become an intense political question, with Republicans looking for ways to provide property tax relief while Democrats arguing for a broader investment in health care and child care support. Parents, friends and employers will be able to deposit as much as $5,000 a year into the Trump accounts, which will be controlled by parents and guardians until their children turn 18, after which the account holder gains control and can use saved-up funds to pay for college, buy a home or start a business. The $1,000 contributions will cost the government $17bn through 2028, according to the Committee for a Responsible Federal Budget. “For decades, Washington politicians gave our children absolutely nothing but debt,” Trump said. “But the Trump accounts, we’re finally seeing and ensuring that every young American gets a great start in life and a really fair shot at the American dream, which is what we all want. They all aspire to that.” Trump increased the national debt by about $8tn in his first term, more than any other president on an inflation-adjusted basis and nearly as much as the government accrued during Barack Obama’s two terms in office. Spending during Trump’s second term is on pace with his first-term mark, with an additional $3bn accrued so far. Inflation currently stands at about 3%, with a decrease of 0.4% last month, driven primarily by a nearly 10% drop in gasoline and oil prices following the short-lived truce in the Iran war. Core inflation was flat for the first time in five years. But gasoline prices have again risen above $4 a gallon in the US, according to AAA tracking, a direct result of oil prices climbing after reawakened hostilities with Iran and the contested strait of Hormuz, a critical waterway for global oil and gas shipments. Trump said he had just come from Dover air force base in Delaware to attend the dignified transfer ceremony for US service members killed in the conflict with Iran. The family of 1st Lt Tyler James Feehan, 25, of Ewa Beach, Hawaii accompanied the president on Air Force One to Georgia and attended the event in Marietta, Trump said. Trump continued to claim that his policies have drawn pledges of $19.2tn into the US economy, a figure that economists from the libertarian Cato Institute and elsewhere consider inflated by illusory investment promises.

topic: California Toddler Bitten Rattlesnake Hiding Amazon

left.title: California toddler bitten by rattlesnake ‘hiding under’ Amazon package

right-center.title: California toddler bitten by rattlesnake hiding beneath Amazon package amid active snake season

farLeftText:
A three-year-old girl landed in the intensive care unit earlier this month after a rattlesnake bit her while she was trying to grab an Amazon package.

Addy, the young girl, ran out with her mother on 13 July to pick up a package dropped over the gate to their home in Santa Clarita, north of Los Angeles.

“Tragically, a southern pacific rattlesnake was hiding under the brown padded envelope and bit Addy’s left hand,” her father, Manny Barajas, wrote in a fundraising campaign note.

Addy’s mother, Lynn, took the girl to meet first responders at a nearby grocery store parking lot, who took her to hospital and administered a dose of anti-venom. The hospital then transferred her to another facility with a pediatric ICU, where she received two more doses of anti-venom, according to Barajas.

The treatment appears to have worked.

“She’s doing great now,” Barajas told KTLA, adding: “She’s still bruised on her left hand where she was bitten. Her spirits are high, her demeanor is great, but she keeps asking why she was bitten by a snake.”

Rattlesnake bites have become more common in California this year, with heatwaves since March increasing snake activity. Three people have died this year, which is about two more than usual. The California poison control system has received reports of 277 bites so far this year, according to the Los Angeles Times – almost as many as it normally received in an entire year.

Rattlesnakes typically avoid humans, but will bite when they feel threatened, cornered or surprised.

California poison control and the Los Angeles county fire department did not immediately return requests for comment.

topic: House Passes Defense Israel 115Tn Despite

left.title: House passes $1.15tn defense bill despite standoff over Israel and Trump’s voter agenda

right-center.title: Chaos erupts as House passes $1.15T defense bill with Israel provision: 'Stop stoking Jew hatred'

farLeftText:
The US House of Representatives passed a $1.15tn defense policy bill in a near-party-line vote on Wednesday, after Democrats refused to back the measure amid the war with Iran and Donald Trump’s push to impose new restrictions on voting nationwide.

A key plank of the president’s plan to spend $1.5tn on the military in the 2027 fiscal year, the National Defense Authorization Act (NDAA), passed the Republican-controlled chamber by a 216-212 vote, with all but six Democrats opposing its passage. Seven House Republicans broke ranks to oppose the measure, which now goes to the Senate.

The NDAA is a mammoth bill with effects across the armed services, and Congress passes it each year with bipartisan margins. This year’s version includes pay raises of 5-7% for the military, as well as the codification of the name “Department of War” for the Pentagon. The bill’s large overall cost is itself a key feature, which top House Republicans say is necessary to make generational investments in the armed forces.

“For the first time in 40 years, we’ve been presented a budget that accounts for the true cost of American deterrence. This topline begins to reverse the damage caused by decades of underinvestment in the US military,” Mike Rogers, the House armed services committee chair, said before the vote.

The NDAA’s path to enactment has been complicated by the president’s decision to join Israel in attacking Iran without first seeking authorization from Congress. Last week, Senate Democrats blocked the advancement of that chamber’s version of the NDAA in protest of the war, which has flared anew after a memorandum of understanding meant to spur peace talks between the US and Iran collapsed.

Democrats also soured on the bill after Mike Johnson, the Republican House speaker, acceded to the demands of rightwing lawmakers and inserted into the legislation the Save America Act, a Trump-backed proposal to ban mail-in ballots and impose new identification restrictions on voters at the polls and when they register.

The party also objected to the overall prospect of allocating $1.15tn to the military while slashing funding elsewhere in the federal government, just a year after Congress’s Republican majorities approved extending an array of lower tax rates indefinitely.

“The annual NDAA used to be a consistently bipartisan, substantive piece of legislation,” said Rosa DeLauro, the top Democratic appropriator. “I am disappointed that under Republican leadership, it has become just another partisan bill that is written to satisfy the most extreme Republican voices rather than a serious, nonpolitical framework for our national defense.”

Adam Smith, the Democratic ranking member on the House armed services committee, had voted to advance the measure out of panel, but opposed its passage on the House floor, saying it had been “loaded with toxic, partisan amendments that continue the rightwing culture war assault on the LGBTQ+ community and preempt state and local laws under the guise of military readiness”.

“Our country faces threats and DoD has modernization needs, but I will not support making massive cuts to critically important domestic programs and slashing taxes to meet those needs,” Smith said.

Amid a wider backlash within the party over US support for Israel, some Democrats said they opposed the NDAA because of its inclusion of provisions that would deepen cooperation between the two countries’ defense industries.

“This amendment is an existential threat to American sovereignty and democracy,” said the progressive congresswoman Alexandria Ocasio-Cortez, who was among the no votes.

The House’s passage of the bill came after the chamber considered dozens of amendments. While many were agreed to unanimously, others offered barometers for how far the Republican-controlled chamber was willing to go on the most divisive issues before the parties.

A proposal by the Republican Eli Crane to halt all military aid to Ukraine was resoundingly defeated by a 76-350 vote, while an amendment from Republican Lauren Boebert to codify Trump’s executive order banning transgender service members failed by a 212-217 tally.

However, the House agreed to amendments from Nancy Mace barring the military health insurer from offering some gender-related care, and banning transgender athletes from girls’ sports at defense department-run schools.

Beyond the NDAA, House Republicans on Wednesday approved a budget plan that will authorize $73bn for the war with Iran while circumventing the Democratic filibuster in the Senate. But thatblueprint falls far short of the $350bn the Pentagon originally proposed to bring overall defense spending to $1.5tn for the upcoming fiscal year.

centerRightText: NEW You can now listen to Fox News articles! The House passed its $1.15 trillion annual defense policy bill Wednesday after a bipartisan revolt over a provision expanding U.S.-Israel defense technology cooperation. The fiscal year 2027 National Defense Authorization Act passed 216-212, with Republicans largely supporting and Democrats mostly opposing the measure. The annual bill, which sets Pentagon policy and typically draws broad bipartisan support, received only six Democratic votes as much of the caucus objected to Section 219 and Republican-backed amendments restricting transgender care and easing access to personal guns on military bases. HOUSE DEMOCRATS FRACTURE BADLY OVER MASSIE AMENDMENT TO CUT $3.3B IN U.S. AID TO ISRAEL Opposition came from a coalition of progressive Democrats and Republican skeptics of U.S. support for Israel, who accused congressional leaders of shielding the provision from scrutiny after lawmakers were denied a separate vote to remove it. The fight erupted as support for Israel has weakened among House Democrats and some "America First" Republicans during the war in Gaza and the widening conflict with Iran. Just one week earlier, 103 Democrats joined Rep. Thomas Massie, R-Ky., in voting to block $3.3 billion in U.S. aid to Israel. For opponents, Section 219 would move the relationship in the opposite direction by embedding U.S.-Israel cooperation more deeply inside the Pentagon’s weapons-development and acquisition system. The provision would direct the war secretary to designate a Pentagon "executive agent" to coordinate and accelerate bilateral defense research, testing and industrial cooperation. Critics argued that structure would make the relationship harder for future administrations or Congresses to unwind and could shift parts of the partnership away from highly visible aid votes into less visible technology, procurement and production arrangements. Supporters said it would coordinate longstanding programs and give the United States faster access to Israeli defense technology. THE US-ISRAEL STRATEGIC ALLIANCE – PARTNERSHIP, NOT CHARITY Rep. Alexandria Ocasio-Cortez, D-N.Y., urged lawmakers to reject the entire defense bill, arguing that the provision would "merge parts of our military with the IDF," referring to the Israel Defense Forces. "This amendment is an existential threat to American sovereignty and democracy," Ocasio-Cortez wrote on X prior to the vote, referring to Section 219. "Every member of Congress must vote NO." Rep. Mike Lawler, R-N.Y., shot back on X, "Good grief, you are either obtuse or intentionally lying. We are not merging our military with the IDF. As we do with many of our allies, we enter into cooperative agreements, share intelligence, develop technology and ammunitions, and conduct military exercises together. Stop stoking Jew hatred and do better." Massie similarly opposed both the rule governing debate and the final bill after Republican leaders denied a floor vote on his bipartisan amendment to strike the provision. "We should not merge our military tech and supply chain with Israel’s," Massie said on X. Beyond creating the executive agent, Section 219 would direct the Pentagon to identify jointly developed or Israeli-origin technologies that could be used in U.S. weapons programs and promote joint ventures, licensing agreements and U.S.-based production partnerships with Israeli companies. Before final passage, the House adopted two amendments from Rep. Anna Paulina Luna, R-Fla. One removed four uses of the word "integration," while the other extended annual reports to Congress for as long as the executive agent remains in place. The original reporting requirement would have ended in 2030. The amendments left the initiative’s central structure and cooperative programs intact, falling short of opponents’ demand that Section 219 be removed entirely. SEN JOHN FETTERMAN PLEDGES TO BE 'LAST MAN STANDING' IN THE DEMOCRATIC PARTY IN SUPPORT OF ISRAEL Massie’s amendment was backed by Democratic Reps. Ro Khanna of California, Jim McGovern of Massachusetts, Jesús "Chuy" García of Illinois and Rashida Tlaib of Michigan. Section 219 would not combine the countries’ command structures, place U.S. troops under Israeli authority or give Israel control over U.S. military operations. The United States and Israel also already cooperate extensively on defense technology, including the Iron Dome, Arrow and David’s Sling missile-defense systems, counter-drone technology and anti-tunnel capabilities. The provision’s principal change would be to place a designated Pentagon executive agent in charge of coordinating and expanding those efforts across the War Department. House Armed Services Committee Chairman Mike Rogers, R-Ala., said the provision would improve oversight and efficiency by assigning responsibility for existing initiatives to a single official. "In no way does it give away command and control of our military operations, personnel or equipment," Rogers said last month. Critics argued that the executive agent structure would give U.S.-Israel projects a privileged path through the Pentagon and further entrench Israeli technology and companies in the American defense supply chain. The fight threatened the broad coalition traditionally needed to pass the NDAA, one of the few major bills Congress has routinely approved with bipartisan support. Democratic opposition was also fueled by Republican amendments restricting gender-affirming medical care for troops and military dependents and transgender participation in sports at Defense Department schools. The House voted 219-208 Tuesday to prohibit Tricare from covering certain gender-transition procedures and treatments, with five Democrats supporting the amendment and one Republican opposing it. Lawmakers separately voted 221-203 to prohibit transgender girls from participating in girls sports at War Department schools. The House rejected a separate attempt by Rep. Lauren Boebert, R-Colo., to codify President Donald Trump’s executive order barring transgender troops from serving in the military. That amendment failed 217-212 after four Republicans joined every Democrat in opposition. Lawmakers also narrowly approved an amendment making it easier for qualified service members and civilian War Department employees to receive permission to carry personal guns on military installations. The measure passed 215-214. An amendment that would have largely prohibited Ukraine security assistance failed, though 76 Republicans supported it. The proposal would have preserved funding only for security at the U.S. Embassy in Kyiv. The underlying NDAA would authorize roughly $1.15 trillion for national defense, including pay raises ranging from 5% to 7% for service members, expanded weapons and munitions production and new investments in missile defense, autonomous systems, hypersonics and other emerging technologies. The bill would also formally change the Department of Defense’s name to the Department of War, deeming references to the defense secretary and department in federal law to mean the secretary and Department of War. The NDAA sets defense policy and authorizes spending levels but does not provide the money itself. Congress must separately pass defense appropriations legislation to fund the programs and priorities included in the measure. Consideration of the legislation had been delayed for three weeks by a separate Republican fight over the SAVE America Act, Trump-backed election legislation requiring documentary proof of citizenship to register to vote and identification to cast a ballot in federal elections. The House approved the election measure in February, but it stalled in the Senate, where Republicans lacked the 60 votes needed to overcome a filibuster. Republican leaders revived the legislation through a procedure directing the House clerk to combine it with the NDAA after passage but before sending the package to the Senate. That approach allowed the House to vote on the defense and election measures separately before transmitting them as one bill. The plan initially collapsed June 30 when 13 Republicans joined Democrats in voting against the procedural rule. Rep. Anna Paulina Luna, R-Fla., and other holdouts later relented after Speaker Mike Johnson promised to attach the election legislation to appropriations bills and other must-pass measures. CLICK HERE TO DOWNLOAD THE FOX NEWS APP The combined defense and election package faces an uncertain future in the Senate, where Democrats blocked consideration of their chamber’s NDAA last week in protest of the Trump administration’s war with Iran. The debate also comes as the Pentagon seeks additional money to cover the conflict and replenish U.S. weapons stockpiles. The White House has requested an $87.6 billion supplemental package, including $67.1 billion for defense needs, while House Republicans are considering additional military and intelligence funding through a party-line reconciliation package.

topic: Inter Miami Casemiro Investigation Sign Announces

left.title: Inter Miami sign Casemiro as MLS announces investigation into tampering

center.title: Inter Miami under investigation after signing Casemiro

farLeftText: Inter Miami have signed Casemiro as a free agent, but Major League Soccer is investigating the South Florida side for potential tampering in the process of acquiring the Brazilian midfielder. Casemiro has signed what amounts to a one-year contract with Miami, beginning now and ending at the conclusion of the 2027 “sprint season” that precedes the league’s switch to a fall-to-spring calendar in 2027-28. There is an option for an extension that would see the Brazilian stay in Miami through the 2028-29 MLS season. Both the club and league announcement said that Inter Miami had acquired the discovery rights to Casemiro from the Los Angeles Galaxy. The league did not specify how or under what terms Miami acquired Casemiro’s discovery priority. “Major League Soccer is reviewing a tampering allegation against Inter Miami CF,” the MLS statement said. “The league is gathering all relevant information and will refrain from further comment until the review is complete. While Inter Miami CF and the LA Galaxy have reached a settlement for the Discovery Priority to sign Casemiro, the terms will be released upon conclusion of the tampering investigation.” Casemiro, a five-time Champions League winner with Real Madrid, recently completed a four-season stint at Manchester United. He played with Brazil at the 2026 World Cup as the Seleçao were eliminated by Norway in the last 16. A strong defensive midfielder, Casemiro joins an Inter Miami side already boasting Lionel Messi, Rodrigo De Paul and Luis Suárez as the stars of a team defending their 2025 MLS Cup title win. In MLS, most major player acquisitions from outside the league are handled via the discovery process. In the discovery process, each team maintains a list of up to five players for whom they have priority to sign should they decide to come to the league. However, that does not mean that only that club can sign that player – MLS clubs can work out deals with each other to transfer the discovery rights from one club to the other, enabling the other to enter into negotiations with the player. Marco Reus, for example, was initially on Charlotte FC’s discovery list when the LA Galaxy sought to sign him in 2024, resulting in the Galaxy paying Charlotte $400,000 for Reus’ discovery rights. MLS’s announcement of the investigation into Inter Miami did not specify whether the tampering charge concerned any negotiations Inter Miami may have had with Casemiro directly before acquiring his discovery rights, or whether it was focused more on the acquisition of the discovery rights. The 34-year-old is considered one of the best midfielders of his generation. The São Paulo native moved to Real Madrid in 2013 and went on to win those five Champions League titles in addition to three La Liga crowns and a Copa del Rey. His biggest success with Manchester United came in winning the 2023-24 FA Cup. skip past newsletter promotion Free newsletter | Twice weekly Sign up to Soccer Desk: World Cup Edition Jonathan Wilson and Guardian journalists bring expert analysis and reporting on the biggest stories in soccer. Preview latest Enter your email Sign up after newsletter promotion “Casemiro embodies everything this club stands for: leadership, a winning mentality, and an extraordinary track record at the highest level of the game,” Inter Miami managing owner Jorge Mas said in the club’s announcement. Inter Miami co-owner David Beckham added: “He is a person and a player that I have admired for a long time. He is a winner who has achieved so much in the game and after such an incredible career with Real Madrid and Manchester United I’m delighted that he’s decided to make Miami his next home.”

centerText:
Major League Soccer says it is investigating allegations of tampering by Inter Miami after the club signed Brazil midfielder Casemiro.

On Wednesday, Miami announced they had signed the 34-year-old - who left Manchester United at the end of last season - on a deal that runs to the end of the 2027 MLS campaign, with an option to extend until June 2029.

But moments later, the MLS released a statement saying it was "reviewing a tampering allegation" against the club.

"The league is gathering all relevant information and will refrain from further comment until the review is complete," it added.

"While Inter Miami CF and the LA Galaxy have reached a settlement for the discovery priority [first rights] to sign Casemiro, the terms will be released upon conclusion of the tampering investigation."

The investigation concerns Casemiro's discovery rights, an MLS regulation that gives a specific club first refusal over a player should they want to join.

A club may have up to five players on their discovery list at any time and can add or remove players.

Los Angeles Galaxy held the discovery rights for Casemiro but in announcing the player's signing, Inter Miami said they had acquired the rights from the Galaxy.

LA Galaxy said in a statement: "The LA Galaxy and Inter Miami CF have reached a settlement for the discovery priority to sign Casemiro, and the terms will be released upon conclusion of the tampering investigation currently open with Major League Soccer."

Casemiro announced in January that he would depart Old Trafford at the end of the 2026-27 season following a mutual decision not to renew his contract.

He won five Champions League titles with Real Madrid before moving to United in 2022, who played for Brazil as they reached the last 16 at this summer's World Cup.

"What motivates me the most, and I think this is true for every player, is winning and continuing to grow," he said.

Casemiro made 160 appearances for United, scoring 26 goals and providing 14 assists as he won one EFL Cup and one FA Cup.

In the wake of Casemiro's departure, United have prioritised midfield reinforcements in this window, buying Andrey Santos for £50m from Chelsea, and Youri Tielemans from Aston Villa for £35m.

Before the announcement of the investigation, Inter Miami co-owner Sir David Beckham said of Casemiro: "He is a winner who has achieved so much in the game and after such an incredible career with Real Madrid and Manchester United I'm delighted that he's decided to make Miami his next home."

At Miami, Casemiro is set to join former Real and Manchester United team-mate Sergio Reguilon as well as Argentina's World Cup finalists Lionel Messi and Rodrigo de Paul.

It is not the first time Inter Miami have been probed over transfer dealings.

In 2021 they were fined $2m after breaking MLS rules with the signing of France midfielder Blaise Matuidi from Juventus the previous year.

The MLS said Matuidi had been paid more than was allowed, effectively giving the team four "designated players" last season rather than the permitted three. Designated players' wages do not count towards the MLS salary cap.

Known as the 'Beckham rule', it was introduced when the ex-England captain joined Los Angeles Galaxy in 2007.

topic: British Woman Jailed Hong Kong Blackmail

left.title: British woman jailed in Hong Kong for blackmail after accusing banker of rape

center.title: British woman jailed for blackmail after accusing banker of rape in Hong Kong

farLeftText: A British woman who reported being raped to police in Hong Kong has been sentenced to six years in prison for blackmail and perverting the course of public justice. Isabel Rose, 26, from south-east London, travelled to Hong Kong in early 2024 to visit a British banker she had met while travelling in south-east Asia. “I came here with four days of clothes, not realising I would be here for two years,” Rose said while out on bail. “Somewhere along the line, quite early on, something went wrong.” The Hong Kong district judge Adriana Noelle Tse Ching said Rose had “not shown an iota of remorse” before handing down the sentence, and called the allegation “completely fabricated”. The events manager, who was first arrested in 2024, maintained throughout the proceedings that she was raped. Radd Seiger, the spokesperson for Rose, said: “Today is obviously a difficult day for Isabel and her mother, Aysha. We will now take advice from the legal team and consider all available options.” In court, Rose, dressed in a black jacket over a white top, looked across at supporters as the judge handed down the sentence. She appeared solemn and gestured to the public gallery as she was escorted out of the dock. The case centred on allegations that Rose demanded money from the British banker, who can not be named for legal reasons, after she accused him of raping her. Rose travelled to Hong Kong in January 2024 after meeting the banker on holiday in Thailand. They stayed in touch and he later invited her to Hong Kong and offered to pay for her flights. Rose said she was raped in his apartment about 12 hours after landing in Hong Kong and reported the allegation to police. Police initially arrested the man but later released him and arrested Rose. She was subsequently charged with blackmail and perverting the course of public justice. Forensic testing to establish the rape was inconclusive. In a 300-page judgment, the judge said she accepted the man’s account and rejected Rose’s evidence. The court heard that Rose had asked for £5,000 after the alleged sexual assault and later demanded £100,000 while threatening to report him to the police, claims Rose denied. Rose said the money was compensation that the man offered to cover flights, accommodation and medical costs. The judge said the escalating demands amounted to blackmail. Rose was released on bail after the arrest but was unable to leave Hong Kong for almost two years while awaiting trial. The family said they have spent tens of thousands of pounds trying to fight the case. Rose has maintained her innocence. View image in fullscreen Aysha Bell, Isabel Rose’s mother, reported the alleged rape to the Metropolitan police in London. The Met said it was ‘unable to pursue relevant lines of enquiry’. Photograph: Fung Hoi Kin/The Guardian The case also focused on WhatsApp messages exchanged between Rose and the man after the alleged assault. In one exchange, Rose wrote: “You violated me last night, I didn’t wanna have sex.” He responded: “I am deeply sorry. I was slightly drunk and I misread your signals, it’s no excuse and I know it doesn’t make it any better.” The man told the court he misunderstood Rose’s use of the word “violated” to refer to an emotional violation rather than an allegation of rape. He said he read the message in two parts. The judge said Rose had deleted some texts and used disappearing messages. She said the banker had been “nothing but kind to the defendant” and Rose had “taken advantage of his kindness, naivety, friendship and romantic interest”. Rejecting calls from Rose’s legal team for a lighter sentence, the judge described her conduct as “wicked” and “evil”. Campaigners said the case raised important questions about how post-sexual assault behaviour was assessed in court. They said unpredictable behaviour was evidence of trauma, not fabrication. Ngozi Fulani, the founder of the London charity Sistah Space, which supports women of African and Caribbean heritage affected by domestic violence and sexual abuse, said: “We live in a world where people think there is a structured way to respond to rape and sexual assault. That is not the case. What has happened here is that a victim has been blamed for being a victim.” Supporters cried as the judge handed down the sentence. Rose’s mother, Aysha Bell, who has spent much of the past two years in Hong Kong supporting her daughter, appeared teary-eyed in court. In 2024, Bell reported the alleged rape to the Metropolitan police in London. UK police can legally investigate sexual assault cases involving British nationals abroad. The Met opened a file, assigned a detective to the case and according to correspondence seen by the Guardian, named the banker as a suspect. A Met spokesperson said: “We take reports of this nature extremely seriously and remain committed to investigating all allegations thoroughly. However, we have been unable to pursue relevant lines of enquiry in relation to this particular allegation due to a lack of jurisdiction in the relevant country. Should circumstances change, we will pursue all relevant enquiries.” The Foreign Office said it was supporting a British woman in Hong Kong and was in contact with her family and the local authorities.
centerLeftText: No article found in this category for this topic.
centerText: According to local press reports, Judge Adriana Tse sentenced her to five years in prison for blackmail and three years for perverting the course of justice, with one year to be served consecutively. Prosecutors argued that Rose fabricated the allegation in an attempt to extort £100,000 ($134,000) from the man, whose identity was withheld. Rose denied the charges throughout the trial and has consistently maintained that she was raped. Rose had travelled to Hong Kong on 31 January 2024 after meeting the man while in Thailand a month earlier, according to court documents. At the centre of the prosecution case were WhatsApp messages exchanged between the pair in the hours and days after the alleged rape on 1 February at his apartment. In one message sent the following morning, Rose wrote: "Are you annoyed I didn't want to have sex," to which the man replied: "What no of course not. Was impressed by your restraint." Later that day, Rose wrote: "You violated me last night, I didn't wanna have sex." He replied: "I'm really deeply sorry, I was slightly drunk and I misread your signals, it's no excuse and I know it doesn't make it any better." In a later exchange, Rose wrote: "You basically raped me." The man replied: "That's not what I'm into at all and I feel sick." The pair later exchanged messages about money. According to the written judgement of the verdict published in March, the man agreed to reimburse Rose for her travel and accommodation. In one message, Rose wrote: "Send me the right amount you owe me for everything. Think about that. Think carefully." He transferred £5,000 that day and tried to send a further £5,000. Rose subsequently wrote: "You've given 10% of what I'm owed. 100% of my soul gone." In a later message, the man wrote: "But I don't have 100k, I have no way of paying you that." Rose reported the rape to police on 3 February. The man was arrested and questioned before being released without charge. Rose was arrested the following day and later charged with blackmail and perverting the course of public justice.

topic: Cockroach Protests Indias Modi Promises Punish

left.title: ‘Cockroach’ protests: India’s Modi promises to punish corrupt officials in bid to quell anger

center.title: India's Modi government under growing pressure as 'cockroach' protests intensify

farLeftText: Narendra Modi has said he will set up “fast track courts” to expedite the punishment of corrupt officials who leaked examination papers,as India’s prime minister seeks to quell the youth-led “cockroach” protest movement that has rocked Delhi and challenged his government. In his first public remarks on the movement, Modi said on X “Nothing is more important than the welfare and future of our youth … Those who try to harm the future of our youth will not be spared.” The offer of faster justice than usual from India’s clogged judicial system, which can take decades to deliver a verdict, was swiftly rejected by the Cockroach Janta party (CJP), the satirical name the youth protest movement coined after India’s top judge called unemployed youths “cockroaches”. “Dharmendra Pradhan must resign,” the CJP said, referring to the education minister. His resignation is a core demand of the movement, along with reform of the education system. An estimated 50,000 people protested in Delhi on Monday, even though police denied them permission to march on parliament and later mounted baton charges and fired teargas. On Wednesday, more than 1,000 people remained camped out at the CJP’s protest site in central Delhi despite the gruelling heat and humidity. Many protesters had travelled for miles to be there. Modi’s statement follows indications the government is softening its stand somewhat. Ministers have been at pains to say they are willing to talk to the CJP but the one thing they refuse to mention is Pradhan’s resigation. The protest site at Jantar Mantar is overflowing with supporters eager to show their solidarity. Unable to fit into the enclosed area, the crowd has spilled outside on to the roads. Some sort out donations of food and water sent by well wishers, others hand out food, including to the police on duty, fan those wilting from the heat, or clear up litter. Young men and women in other cities across India have also voiced their support for the CJP’s demands by holding their own demonstrations. At a press conference on Wednesday, the opposition leader Rahul Gandhi escalated the rhetoric, demanding that apart from Pradhan resigning, Modi should apologise for failing to provide a fair education system. View image in fullscreen Demonstrators hold anti-Modi placards during a protest. Photograph: Ashish Vaishnav/SOPA Images/Shutterstock Gandhi said the country had seen 152 exam paper leaks over the past decade without resulting in a single conviction. “Somebody, or a group of people, are destroying our education system, India’s most valuable asset, destroying the lives of our students. What does the government do? Zero convictions,” he said. If anything, the movement is hardening its stance after Monday’s brutal crackdown by the police. CJP leaders were deeply disappointed when their meeting on Monday with the health minister, J. P. Nadda – the first in over 60 days – resulted in nothing. Its founder, Abhijeet Dipke, has said they will not go to the government. If ministers want to speak to them, they will have to come to the protest site and talk to them in public, he said.
centerLeftText: No article found in this category for this topic.
centerText: On Monday, tens of thousands of protesters flooded central Delhi after the CJP called for a march to parliament as the monsoon session began. At least 60 people were injured after police used batons and tear gas to disperse the crowds. Officers also dismantled the stage and makeshift tents at Jantar Mantar, the protest's main base near the 300-year-old observatory. But the demonstrators have refused to leave, maintaining a round-the-clock sit-in. A day after the violence, they were visited by several senior opposition leaders such as former Delhi chief minister Arvind Kejriwal and veteran politician Sharad Pawar. Later on Tuesday, Congress president Mallikarjun Kharge and Gandhi led a surprise march to Modi's house, demanding the resignations of Pradhan, Home Minister Amit Shah who the Delhi police report to and PM Modi. Gandhi said the government must apologise to students for the crackdown, adding that the Congress decided to stage a protest outside Modi's house because they weren't allowed to raise the issue in the parliament. He said he had met speaker Om Birla to seek a discussion, but "the speaker said he needed to take permission from the government, but it was very clear to us that there was no interest in the government of having a debate". Federal Minister Jitendra Singh denied Gandhi's allegation, external - he said he informed the Congress MP that the government had accepted his demand for a discussion but alleged that Gandhi later also demanded the resignation of Pradhan. He accused Gandhi of "changing the goalpost" and of refusing to end the protest despite being told that the place wasn't conducive to such demonstrations.

topic: Iran School Killed Strike Trump Images

left.title: Trump Says Images of Bombed Iran School Could Be "AI" But Study Confirms U.S. Killed 140+ in Strike

center.title: 'What was their crime?': BBC visits Iran school where strike killed 120 children

farLeftText:
This is a rush transcript. Copy may not be in its final form.

AMY GOODMAN: The Trump administration has repeatedly refused to take responsibility for the school strike. President Trump recently appeared on Fox News and was questioned by Trey Yingst about the strike.

TREY YINGST: Democrats released a letter calling on your administration to unveil the findings of the investigation into a strike that killed more than a hundred children on the first day of the war. Will you commit to releasing the findings of this investigation?

PRESIDENT DONALD TRUMP: Well, I’d have to speak to the generals that are doing the — because we mostly have generals. You know, I don’t think anybody’s going to ever be able to say what happened there.

TREY YINGST: There are images from this site that show fragments that appear to be from U.S. Tomahawk missiles. Is it possible that old intelligence or a mistake during a very active time in the war led to this event?

PRESIDENT DONALD TRUMP: It is, but it’s also possible that those images that you have are AI-generated. … Now, I know we’re waiting for a conclusive report. I don’t think there can be a conclusive report. There are missiles —

TREY YINGST: Will you release the report when it is finished?

PRESIDENT DONALD TRUMP: I guess so. I mean, I have to ask the military people. There might be a reason why they can’t.

AMY GOODMAN: While President Trump claimed evidence of the school strike may be AI-generated, Forensic Architecture is out with a new report revealing new details about the U.S. bombing. Joining us from London will be a Forensic Architecture researcher. But first, Sky News recently released its own major report on the school strike, based in part on the research of Forensic Architecture. One mother, Khadija Ahmedizadeh, spoke to Sky News about her son Mohammad Taha, who died in the attack.

KHADIJA AHMEDIZADEH: [translated] I collapsed onto the ground on my hands and knees. I said, “Mohammad Taha will choke. For God’s sake, pull him out quickly.” I picked up a stone and said, “I’ll kill myself with this if you don’t bring my son out, quickly, before he chokes!”

It was one month after. We were told that Mohammad Taha had been found. I thought they would show him to me. But when we went, I saw that a child I had raised for 10 years with so much effort was now reduced to the size of a newborn baby, wrapped in a shroud. They gave him to me and said, “Here, this is your child.” I didn’t see my child’s face again.

After one month, Mohammad Taha was laid to rest next to his classmates. I spent that whole night at the cemetery. I was scared. Mohammad Taha was afraid of the dark, too.

They tore my son to pieces. What should I do now? Who am I supposed to hold responsible for this? Who do I have any power over?

AMY GOODMAN: We’re joined now in London by Samaneh Moafi, assistant director of research at Forensic Architecture, the new report titled “Airstrikes on Minab Primary School.”

This is very painful, Samaneh, because we’re talking about real life. We’re talking about well over 160 children and some adults who were killed on that first day when the U.S. struck Minab. You also hear President Trump saying, “We don’t know actually what happened,” as he’s questioned by Fox, and it may be “AI-generated.” You have done the research. What exactly did you find?

SAMANEH MOAFI: I want to take one step before, and I want to talk about where Minab is. Minab is in the south of Iran along the Strait of Hormuz. The war in 2026 started from the very site where the Iran-Iraq War was brought to an end. In 1988, the United States had escalated its military operations against the oil infrastructure and naval bases in Iran. And amongst those strikes, it also shut down an Iranian passenger flight by the Strait of Hormuz and attributed it to a misidentification. All 290 passengers on that flight were killed. This is why, for many Iranians, the attack on Shajareh Tayyebeh primary school echoed the downing of that passenger flight 38 years earlier. It was a nation’s trauma being relived. Some of the preliminary reports had said that the attack on the school, like the passenger flight, was the result of a misidentification, a targeting mistake.

In our investigation, we used satellite imagery to show that at the time of the attack, the building was a school and had served that purpose for over a decade. By October 2015, all architectural indicators of military presence, a watchtower or anything, were destroyed. And new entrances were built. New perimeter walls were built around the school. By 2017, we see a football pitch and assembly lines being painted on the playgrounds. By 2018, we see the walls being painted in blue and pink to identify the girls’ school and the boys’ school. We see children playing in the playgrounds of these — in the satellite imagery. We were able to verify also that many of the buildings in the immediate surrounding of the school were visibly civilian — a clinic, a cultural center, a cafe, a supermarket, a car wash. Many of these facilities maintained active social media accounts, so they were publishing and posting about what is happening in them. Some of these were used by the school. The culture center was — offered space for children for their ceremonies, award ceremonies, so on and so forth.

It’s true that, you know, during the Iran-Iraq War, this site was used as a military base. In the 1980s, CIA documents also refer to a possible military support facility in Minab, laying the ground kind of for like a target, you know? But the Sky News documentary that featured this investigation showed that these targets had not been checked properly for years and years.

I can also tell you how further we went. We looked into the photographs and videos that were published on the school’s website, publicly. These were images of ceremonies, say, the Mother’s Day ceremony, the Nowruz celebrations that marked the new year, the book fairs. We use these images and videos to reconstruct the building of the school as a kind of a navigable 3D model. Some of these images belong to January, only a few weeks before the strikes had happened. Some of the children we saw in those images — Mohammad Taha was one of them — were killed during the strike. The digital model that we built went into as much detail as interior finishes of every single classroom, the decorations on the walls, the bright paint on the staircase that linked the assembly hall of the boys’ school on the ground floor to the girls’ school on the top floor. We checked every room. There was no hidden room for any kind of a use. It was a school for — you know, for children.

AMY GOODMAN: And if you can say — apparently, this according to CNN, the investigation into the U.S. strike that hit the school in Iran has sat for months with the military command, while leaders have held off on ordering a critical standard intelligence review. And we also know about the cutting of the agencies that would review the target sites. In this last minute we have, Samaneh Moafi, the most important evidence you felt you had concluding it was a U.S. Tomahawk missile?

SAMANEH MOAFI: We looked at images. There were images and videos available from the time of the strikes. The first nine strikes started at around 11:20 and across one-and-a-half minute. The first strike landed only 130 meters away from the school. The fourth strike landed 10 seconds after on the school. Mohammad Taha, the 9-year-old you were mentioning in the beginning, was playing in the schoolyard when the first strike begins, and he rushes to the safety of his classroom. And that’s when the school is hit.

Videos of the aftermath of these strikes show that families are on site. Aid workers are on site. They’re rescuing the children under the rubble. These images are from 12:30, 1:15 p.m., like around noon. A number of children were rescued at this time. Parastesh Zaeri, the 11-year-old child, was amongst them. From as early as 1:45, or around that time, multiple Iranian news channels are reporting on the attacks. They’re saying this was — there was an attack on a girls’ school. They’re saying there was death toll of five children, there is more coming, so on and so forth.

AMY GOODMAN: We have 30 seconds.

SAMANEH MOAFI: As families and rescuers are on site, a second wave of strikes are hit. And it’s in these ones that we do see the profile of a Tomahawk. And this is owned — the only party within the war that has this missile is the U.S. Following the initial media reportings, the U.S. forces would have had ample opportunity to reassess their initial misidentification before conducting the next round of strikes. But it’s simple: They didn’t.

AMY GOODMAN: Samaneh Moafi, I want to say thank you very much for being with us, assistant director of research at Forensic Architecture. We’ll link to your new report, “Airstrikes on Minab Primary School.”

That does it for our show. I’ll be in Martha’s Vineyard for the screening of Steal This Story, Please!, July 31st. I’m Amy Goodman.
centerLeftText: No article found in this category for this topic.
centerText:
The school day had begun as usual, Amaineh says.

Because it was the Islamic holy month of Ramadan, the children gathered inside the prayer hall to recite the Quran, she recalls, before she began teaching her pupils a new letter of the Persian alphabet.

But, as the first attacks of the war unfolded across Iran, she says the headteacher announced the school would be closing, and teachers began contacting parents, urging them to collect their children.

Only five of Amaineh's 15 first-grade pupils were left in her classroom on the upper floor, she says, when she first heard an explosion not far from the school. She says she was in the corridor and rushed back for the children.

"Just as I went to leave, a missile struck the school," says Amaineh. "Something hit my head and my students' heads - we were all sent flying."

Then, she says, thick black smoke filled the classroom: "I couldn't even see the pupils I was holding in my arms. We couldn't breathe."

She says she remembers hearing another explosion, as well as the sounds of screaming, children crying and then the school itself collapsing – "destroyed all at once".

"Blood was pouring from me and the students," she adds.

topic: Arizona 2020 Election Deniers Trumpbacked Candidates

left-center.title: ‘It’s a stone around your neck’: Arizona Republicans once again nominate 2020 election deniers

center.title: Trump-backed candidates, 2020 election deniers, win Arizona primaries

farLeftText: No article found in this category for this topic.
centerLeftText: No article found in this category for this topic.
centerText: Trump-backed candidates, 2020 election deniers, win Arizona primaries toggle caption Ross D. Franklin/AP Arizona voters have picked their party nominees for governor and two competitive congressional races, as the primary election outcome provides perspective on how voters are thinking in a key swing state. President Trump-backed candidates prevailed over other Republicans in the race for governor and a key U.S. House seat. GOP voters also chose candidates for governor and secretary of state who have openly opposed the 2020 presidential election results in a state that has been at the center of debates over election security and integrity. U.S. Rep. Andy Biggs is the Republican nominee for governor. After the 2020 election, he refused to certify the results over Trump's unverified claims that the election was stolen for former President Joe Biden. For Arizona's secretary of state, its top elections officer, GOP voters nominated Alexander Kolodin, an attorney who participated in lawsuits challenging the 2020 election results. Sponsor Message Both governor and secretary of state can be key positions in asserting power over the election process in the battleground state. While Trump won the state in 2024, Arizonans elected a Democratic governor two years before that. Trump's popularity in Arizona has also dwindled over his handling of the economy. But that didn't stop Republican voters from picking Trump-endorsed candidates as the nominee in top races. Of the 18 congressional districts across the country that are considered true toss-ups, two of them are in Arizona. Democrats are aiming to flip those two congressional seats from red to blue, both critical battles for the Democratic Party if it wants to have a shot at taking back the House in November. Trump-backed candidate faces Democratic incumbent for the governor's office Republican voters selected a Trump-endorsed conservative, Biggs, to challenge Democratic Gov. Katie Hobbs for the role in November. Biggs beat three other Republican candidates, including a bid from U.S. Rep. David Schweikert, who was considered to be the more pragmatic, moderate candidate. Biggs is a longtime Trump loyalist who was a party leader in the ultra-right House Freedom Caucus. Biggs, though, has attempted to tamper down his conservative persona during the primary election campaign by painting himself as someone who could appeal to independent voters. Sponsor Message On the Democratic side, Hobbs is considered to be a vulnerable candidate. Hobbs narrowly defeated conservative Republican Kari Lake for governor in 2022. Despite her endangered status, Hobbs has experience coalescing swing voters in a race against an ultra-conservative, MAGA-aligned challenger. A Biggs victory appears to indicate that Republican primary voters are swayed by Trump's opinion, even if the candidate may have a tougher time convincing swing voters ahead of the general election. Cook Political Report labeled the race as a toss-up ahead of the primary, but following the results switched its analysis to reflect a slight Democratic advantage. Candidates locked in on key toss-up races The Trump-endorsed candidate, former NFL kicker Jay Feely, won the Republican nomination in one of the nation's most competitive elections. Feely's Democratic challenger hadn't been determined as of late Tuesday night. The wide-open seat in Arizona's 1st Congressional District will help determine which party holds control of the House. Republicans in the district, which consists of the Phoenix metro area, picked the more moderate candidate out of the two front-runners. Feely beat hard-line Republican former state lawmaker Joseph Chaplik, who used to be a member of the Arizona legislature's Freedom Caucus and had won the support of President Trump. On the Democratic side, Amish Shah is leading former broadcast journalist Marlene Galan-Woods, a candidate backed by the Democratic Congressional Campaign Committee. Galan-Woods had been endorsed by Sen. Mark Kelly, one of the most well-known Democrats in the state. She also switched her party affiliation from Republican to Democrat in 2018. But the race is anyone's seat. Trump carried the district by three points in 2024, and Biden won the district by less than two points in 2020. Whoever wins in November will replace outgoing Republican Rep. Schweikert, who lost the GOP nomination for Arizona governor. Another toss-up race is in Arizona's 6th Congressional District. Voters will pick between Republican Rep. Juan Ciscomani and Democrat JoAnna Mendoza in November. Both candidates ran unopposed in their respective primary elections. Sponsor Message Ciscomani was first elected in 2022, but won his reelection bid in 2024 with 50% of the vote. Mendoza is a military veteran who served two tours in Iraq and one in Afghanistan. The district covers parts of Tucson, a sliver of the southern border and rural communities across southeastern Arizona. It's considered by Cook Political Report to be one of the most evenly divided districts in the nation. Trump won the district by less than a point in 2024 and lost by that same margin in 2020. In Arizona's blue-leaning 4th Congressional District, Democratic incumbent Rep. Greg Stanton fended off a primary challenge by progressive candidate Kai Newkirk.
